prompt: What is Simon Willison's framework for understanding LLMs?
response: Simon Willison's frame on LLMs is "imitation intelligence." Both in terms of imitation meat - not quite the real thing, a bit off in a way that makes you a bit queasy - but also the process that it follows: imitating what it's seen before. Intelligence entirely through imitation. Lots of people do imitation intelligence in general - not real reasoning, the facsimile of it.

prompt: What is the key principle for working effectively with LLMs according to "last principles thinking"?
response: When working with LLMs, think about the superficial, last principles first, then work backwards to the first principles. LLMs are all about vibes of what they've seen, the superficial optics. So if you want to control LLMs don't try to engage at first principles, engage at the superficial optics first. Meet LLMs where they are. The LLM's intermediate representation should be a format the LLM is maximally familiar with.

prompt: Why might people fear their data being used by LLMs more than generic cloud services?
response: It's kind of wild how much people care about their data flowing to LLMs more so than to a generic cloud services. Generic cloud services could do whatever they want with your data… send it on to other companies, store it in insecure locations, sell it to data brokers. But for some reason there's a visceral fear over and above a generic cloud service. Maybe it's the same invisible fear that your data will somehow get absorbed into a future LLM and show up some how that harms or embarrasses you in the future. A diffuse, invisible threat is more scary.

prompt: What are the main limitations of current hallucinated mini-apps?
response: Hallucinated mini-apps today have two significant problems. 1) They can't safely work with your data. The hallucinated apps all start off with no data in them. They also don't have any sandboxing to speak of… you should be very wary about putting data into any of them. 2) They can't compose with other mini-apps. That means the ceiling of functionality is the biggest mini-app that can fit in an LLM's understanding. The LLM's capability sets a ceiling on what can be done.

prompt: What's more effective for prompting LLMs - complex prompts or curated examples?
response: For prompting, curated examples are more important than a well-crafted complex prompt. A complex prompt has to be interpreted, and can only include what you said. Well curated examples captures the vibe of what you want, even the things you didn't think to call out. With larger context windows, there's more space for examples than before.

prompt: What makes ELI5 (Explain-It-Like-I'm-5) documents so effective for creating clarity?
response: ELI5 documents are unreasonably effective at producing clarity because writing one requires you to distill a fractally complicated thing into a very simple, 2-page frame. You have to distill the underlying thing to the point of caricature. But the caricature is much easier for people to grab on to. The caricature that everyone can grab onto provides a backbone to hang details off of that previously would have been lost in a swirl.

prompt: What's the difference between satisficing and maximizing metrics, and when should you use each approach?
response: When there are metrics everyone defaults into assuming maximization of them, but for the majority of them, you want to satisfice - that is, simply clear the bar. Maximizing most metrics past the good enough bar has strongly diminishing returns, and has a huge opportunity cost. Set a 'green zone' target on a metric, then once you reach the green zone, you stop working on it. This should happen automatically, no need for a meeting.

prompt: Why is it impossible to design a perfect API in isolation?
response: It's impossible to design a perfect API in a vacuum. A useful API is a negotiation, a mutual process of discovery with your ecosystem. This property means that it is fundamentally uncertain. If you wait until an API is 'perfect' to ship, you will ship nothing, or you will ship a thing that if you get even a single detail wrong it will not work. You have to ship an API that is good and useful and then clean it up and evolve it in response with the ecosystem.

prompt: What's the relationship between storytelling and power in organizations and society?
response: The entity that tells the best story gets the most power. Power is largely based on the beliefs of others about who has power. People then make decisions on how to act based on who they believe has power, which gives the people they believe have power actual power. Viral, interesting, distinctive stories spread further and transmit more power.

prompt: Why do people choose to buy from established brands rather than trying new alternatives?
response: People don't buy a brand because it's good, it's because they know the floor. By buying from a brand you've capped the downside.

prompt: What's the advantage of an assistant that gives you multiple options to choose from rather than taking action automatically?
response: This kind of assistant is very forgiving; they don't take actions on your behalf. The human judgment is in the loop, always. But the boring / hard part is automated away. For example, one of the big benefits of a wedding planner is not that you offload judgment decisions to them, but that you know they'll come back with a handful of reasonable (in terms of quality and cost) options to pick from.

prompt: Why do open systems eventually beat proprietary systems in the long run?
response: Open systems start slow but win in the end because innovation is combinatorial. Each unrelated discovery expands our possibility space by increasing the inventory of components we can combine to create something new. It all comes down to: which system can try more combinations more quickly? A system that sums the investment of all participants will beat a system invested in by only a single participant.

prompt: What makes full automation so difficult to achieve with current AI systems?
response: Full automation requires perfect reliability. Full automation requires a human to not be in the loop. Perfect reliability requires a closed loop system that can learn. LLMs today are an open loop. The queries might be saved and used in some fuzzy way to train future iterations of the model, but they don't affect the LLM as it exists. The more general the tasks that users are expected to hand off to the system, the harder it is to be perfectly reliable.

prompt: What is "jagged intelligence" in the context of large language models?
response: The word I came up with to describe the strange, unintuitive fact that state of the art LLMs can both perform extremely impressive tasks (e.g. solve complex math problems) while simultaneously struggle with some very dumb problems. LLMs are like system 1: a general approach that is surprisingly good at vibes-based processing for everything, but not particularly great at anything.

prompt: Why is it so challenging for companies to transition from proprietary to open systems?
response: At some point the rest of the ecosystem will catch up. When your previously differentiated proprietary technology becomes a commodity, you'll be trapped. Getting off the island is a massive one-time cost, and that cost rises the longer you stay on the island. If you do get off the island, you might not make it, and will be actively behind the rest of the ecosystem, at a disadvantage. Everything will feel weird and foreign and hard to understand. You'll have to shift your basis of competition to something else.

prompt: What is the term 'slop' used for in AI contexts?
response: A term I love for unwanted AI-generated content: slop. The term has an adjacency to spam, while being distinct from it. The connotations are pitch perfect: mass produced technically edible junk fit for animals, not humans.

prompt: What are the two approaches to building a car, and which is better?
response: There are two ways to build a car. Tired: Wheels, then add a chassis, then an engine. Not viable until the end. No feedback on if it actually works, or if any incremental change works, until the end. Wired: build a skateboard, then make it a scooter, then a bike, then a car. Continuously viable. Continuous feedback about how to improve it and if incremental tweaks work. A continuously viable solution is many, many orders of magnitude more valuable than a solution that is only viable at the end, because it is significantly more likely to produce something.

prompt: What is the best standard to compare LLMs against when evaluating their performance?
response: If you ask an expert if LLMs are good at a task, they'll say it's insufficient. But what you should do is ask the person who isn't good at the task if it's better than them! You rarely actually have the relevant expert on hand. The standard to beat is the best available human.

prompt: What are the three distinct meanings of the word 'privacy'?
response: There are at least three distinct meanings of the word 'privacy'. The first is compliance - the realm of contracts and regulations that affect the enterprise. The second is anti-surveillance - this uses tools like end-to-end-encryption, security defense in depth, confidential computing, on device computation, etc. The last is contextual integrity - roughly, 'data is used in line with my interest and intent'. The last one is the platonic ideal. You can never fully reach it in all cases. But you can clear a good enough bar for most cases, and you can continue to improve it.

prompt: What is the relationship between perfectionism and continuous viability?
response: Perfectionism prevents continuous viability. 'That's not perfect, so we shouldn't do it.' But perfection is impossible, and in the meantime you're frozen and can't do anything. Different solutions can be better or worse than others. Just because perfection is impossible doesn't mean improvement is impossible, too. The right answer when you come across that kind of nihilism is to say: 'You're right, it's not perfect! But it's better than the alternatives in use today and it is viable today.'

prompt: What are the three main technologies that make up Web Components?
response: Shadow DOM, custom elements, and templates. Shadow DOM allows you to encapsulate things within DOM. Custom elements basically allows you to define your own tags. And templates just allows you to define these bits of document you're going to need again and again.

prompt: What problem does Shadow DOM solve in web development?
response: Shadow DOM is the piece that just allows you to encapsulate things within DOM. We noticed we had a whole bunch of cruft in the DOM to make it look right on screen. And you still need that. But we don't want you to sort of see that when you poke through the DOM. We want you to see something that's more semantic, that's more clear about what you actually mean. And if we allow you to hide some of that extra cruft off to the side, well then you can make it so you only have one hierarchy, which is DOM itself, and not have this bizarro world off to the side.

prompt: How do styles work within Shadow DOM components?
response: A style that's inside of a template, inside of the shadow DOM, will by default only affect things inside of that shadow DOM. The things outside of here are not affected. So that sort of makes sure that your styles that you define in your component don't accidentally go through and mess everything else up. But also, by default, styles that hit your component won't come through. There's a shield. It shields the document styles from you.

prompt: What is the current state of web development that Web Components aims to address?
response: The web platform today is like this highway. It's got a lot of really cool technologies that generally work together pretty well. And as long as you're staying on the highway or cruising along, you can do some really cool stuff really quickly. But then you run into a pothole. There's a piece that's missing that doesn't do exactly what you want it to do. And you say, that's okay. I have JavaScript. I can fix this problem myself. And so you write a little bit of script. You go a little bit off-road. But then you're off-road, and you're trucking along, and you come up to a boulder. And so what ends up happening is you end up going all the way off-road. So these little potholes become giant, heaping pits of despair.

prompt: What advantages do Web Components offer over current JavaScript frameworks?
response: All the frameworks today do this. They all do it for you when they present very nice, clean abstractions about what's going on in your document. But they can't do it in the browser. This is the browser doing it itself, which means it can go faster because it's native code. And you also don't need a build step. You just ship it down to the browser and it just works. Also, if everyone is building on the same foundations, these same primitives, that means that widgets from different libraries will be compatible.

prompt: What's the difference between pushing a rock uphill versus skiing downhill as strategic approaches?
response: If you're pushing a rock up a hill (pushing against the natural gradient of entropy) then you have to carefully plan every move and never slip up. But if you're skiing down hill (going with the natural gradient of entropy), you still have to make decisions about your route and respond to conditions... but the default is unless you get stuck in a dead end you will get there eventually. If you can choose between a push-a-rock-up-a-hill strategy and a ski-down-a-hill strategy, always pick the latter.

prompt: Why is it dangerous when you get very close to being infallible?
response: The downside of thinking you're infallible goes up the closer to infallible you get. When you're very far, it's very clear to everyone that you're not infallible, and they'll take actions to hedge. This hedging creates resilience because it caps the downside. But when you're very close, you start believing it, as does everyone else. And then in the times you don't hit it, it's dangerous because it will surprise you and others in a big way.

prompt: What makes Unix utilities timeless despite software's tendency to become obsolete quickly?
response: The best examples of timeless software are things like Unix utilities. The reason they can be timeless despite all of this is they focus on doing one contained thing, and use one consistent, flexible, well-designed abstraction to interact with everything else: a pipe / file. Unix's 'everything is a file' is like the lego dot: a timeless abstraction that allows pockets of timelessness in a constantly evolving larger system.

prompt: What's the key difference between autopoietic and allopoietic systems?
response: Inside-out things are alive. Outside-in things are engineered. Autopoietic things tend to be self-maintaining. They resist entropy on their own. Allopoietic things cannot be self-maintaining; they need the effort of others to maintain them. This means that autopoietic systems are the ones that build allopoietic systems. The only local pockets of order in the universe, that successfully (and temporarily!) resist the ravages of entropy, are autopoietic systems.

prompt: Why does optional feedback work better than mandatory feedback in creative organizations?
response: When you get mandatory feedback, you might feel coerced into following it even if you don't agree with it. And if you're doing it from extrinsic pressure and not intrinsic alignment, you'll drag your feet and implement it poorly. When the feedback is truly optional, you can treat it not as a threat to defend against, but a gift you have the option of accepting. This allows you to go from 'the thing they're missing is...' to 'the thing they're seeing is...'. When you get to apply your own agency, you can commit to it much more deeply. Paradoxically, having the choice to adopt the feedback often leads to it being adopted in a more powerful and resonant way.

prompt: What's the difference between execution and creation in work?
response: Execution and creation are very different. When it's a project that is your vision, you get to exercise both muscles. When it's someone else's vision, you only get good at execution. The machine doesn't want you to have your own vision - that creates chaos when everyone does it. The machine only wants you to be good at execution. You have to be the one to want to be creative.

prompt: How do LLMs compare to human magicians when it comes to prompting?
response: When wizards prompt an LLM, it's like a magic trick. People watch the results and they look like magic. But then you show them the prompt you used to do it, and the onlooker goes, '... oh, that's it?' and feel almost disappointed. But unlike a magician, there is real magic going on. It's just that the LLMs are what is magic. The wizard just knows how to marshall that magic effectively.

prompt: What makes something transition from requiring constant effort to being self-sustaining?
response: When a thing is viable and coherent it self coheres. Until that point it has to have someone input a huge amount of continuous effort and force of will until it gets to that point. Default cohering vs default decohering - a seemingly small difference that is actually an infinite difference. Does each incremental bit of effort around the thing build it up or diffuse/erode it? Most things in the world are default-decohering.

prompt: Why do organizations tend to resist innovative ideas even when they might be valuable?
response: Organizations optimize for order, not for truth. Because if it goes against the grain, it's potentially dangerous - it could erode the hard-won order and structure. There's a reason there's a grain: the grain works. It has at least worked in the past, and is known to be viable. Going against the grain has to prove not only that it is valuable but that it is viable. As an individual, it's significantly more downside than upside to try an against-the-grain idea. So the emergent equilibrium is individuals in the organization working to maintain order, not truth.

prompt: What's the key difference between having taste and having good vibes?
response: Vibes and taste are distinct. LLMs are great at vibes - they're computers for vibes. But LLMs are terrible at taste. LLMs are high on vibes, low on taste. Taste requires you to be good at vibes, but vibes are not sufficient. Taste is mastery of your own personal vibes. But if your personal vibes are 'everyone's thoughts ever' then it's just the boring centroid.

prompt: What's the difference between how built things and living things handle stress and adaptation?
response: A built thing, like a building, has to be designed by someone to be able to carry a load. It needs someone else to carry in their mind a complete conception of it that is viable in the real world. A living thing like a tree can sense where a branch is swaying and build up more lignin there to help resist the sway. The tree doesn't even need to have a full conception of itself, it just does the obvious thing in the local situated sub-context, and a coherent whole emerges, automatically. Living things adapt for free. Built things cannot adapt themselves.

prompt: Why are UX changes more forgiving than API changes?
response: UX is for humans, and humans are alive. APIs are for code, and code is built. Code can't adapt itself to integrate with an API; a human can adapt themselves for a changed UX interface. There's an entity with agency at each time step of interaction; the human is always in the loop with UX, while with code the human is often only indirectly / rarely in the loop.

prompt: What makes it difficult to understand how a black box system works?
response: You understand a system when your mental model can accurately predict what it will do in response to most inputs. With a black box you don't know what state it's in at any given point and have to guess. And as more things happen it drifts away from the previous state you thought it was, making it very hard to "catch up" your understanding of its state to what it's actually in. Two things that make it hard to synchronize your mental model with how a system actually works: 1) A complex internal state (how many distinct states can it be in?) and 2) Very few externally-visible signals of what state it's in.

prompt: How does the mindset of expecting to make decisions affect learning?
response: When we expect to make a decision, we are in a massively different head space. When we are passively watching something, our brains can wander with no real downside. But when we expect to have to make a decision (e.g. in a game, or when executing in our job), we are constantly absorbing all relevant information, balancing across a lot of possibilities, developing an intuitive predictive model, and being ready to have a decision if someone said "OK, go time, make your decision RIGHT NOW". The latter has orders of magnitude higher rate of knowhow acquisition: much faster learning rate. This is why simulations, games, and play are so good for learning.

prompt: What's the difference between diminishers and illuminators in human interaction?
response: In any collection of humans, there are diminishers and there are illuminators. Diminishers are so into themselves, they make others feel insignificant. They stereotype and label. If they learn one thing about you, they proceed to make a series of assumptions about who you must be. Illuminators, on the other hand, have a persistent curiosity about other people. They have been trained or have trained themselves in the craft of understanding others. They know how to ask the right questions at the right times — so that they can see things, at least a bit, from another's point of view. They shine the brightness of their care on people and make them feel bigger, respected, lit up.

prompt: What is the 'doorbell in the jungle' pattern and how does it work?
response: The doorbell in the jungle pattern involves making a tiny investment - like installing a doorbell in an unlikely place - just in case a miracle happens, then continuously and incrementally responding to any demand that shows up. Instead of jumping in with two feet and building everything all at once, you make teeny investments with basically no downside but large and uncapped upside. The cost of installing that doorbell is basically nothing, but if traffic miraculously shows up, you can transact with that traffic for large profit. From there, you can incrementally invest more and more, in response to concrete, as opposed to theoretical, demand.

prompt: Why do horizontal approaches to building products often seem counterintuitive to business leaders?
response: Everyone knows that Serious Business People have plans, and the more numbers and figures attached to those plans, the more Serious they are. Serious Business People carefully survey the landscape, model possible outcomes, pick the best plan among various alternatives, and then commit to rallying the entire organization around that plan. This vertical approach feels concrete, heroic, and certain. No one ever got fired for being a Serious Business Person. In contrast, the horizontal approach looks loosey-goosey and not very serious, like the practitioner is just happening to get lucky. But while any specific miraculous outcome is indeed luck, the likelihood of a miraculous outcome is quite high, because the practitioner has been deliberately increasing their stochastic luck surface area.

prompt: How does Product Market Fit relate to highly motivated early users?
response: A product has Product Market Fit (PMF) if the value you unlock is greater than the cost of building the product in the first place. We typically think of PMF as if it's some global threshold. But the PMF threshold is tied to a specific population: the 'market'. If you can define the 'market' down to a very small collection of highly motivated users, then even a seemingly low-quality offering can have PMF, just with a very small but highly motivated 'market'. If the amount of investment you'd have to make is small, the smallest viable 'market' that makes it worth doing can be very small indeed.

prompt: What role do natural barriers play in validating customer demand?
response: The jungle created a kind of naturally-occurring gauntlet: a challenging sequence of steps that potential customers had to navigate. The customers that made it through the gauntlet had crawled through bramble bushes, which demonstrated that they were extremely motivated. Those self-selecting users demonstrated the tiny critical mass of initial demand. Whenever you have extremely motivated users, it's likely there's even more demand that is just barely below the current user's pain tolerance bar. If you reduce the amount of bramble bushes the customers have to crawl through, then you'll unlock more demand because you'll allow a larger set of users with slightly lower motivation to become customers.

prompt: How can organizations optimize for serendipity in product development?
response: The doorbell in the jungle pattern is an example of optimizing for serendipity. It would be a miracle if someone hacked through the jungle to ring that doorbell — but it's very low cost to get positive exposure to that miracle so why not do it? By following the doorbell in the jungle pattern, you cultivate positive exposure to a diverse set of miracles. Done well, you can practically guarantee a miraculous result. All you have to do is let go of a bit of the illusion of control and certainty. If the doorbells are cheap enough you can install lots of them in different places, allowing you to react to concrete demand from a diversity of possible directions instead of having to guess where the theoretical demand will come from.

prompt: What is "Voldemorting" and how does it work as a tactic to avoid algorithmic censorship?
response: "Voldemorting" is a tactic people use to avoid algorithmic censorship. You talk in ways that will be very clear to the listener about what you mean, but that are oblique enough that the algorithm doesn't notice. Apparently in China people will find characters that *look* like the word you're trying to say, but don't *sound* like it. But communication only works if the receiver can recover the intended signal, so these indirections have to be obvious enough for a receiver to understand. And particularly effective he-who-must-not-be-named phrases will become used more often (since they effectively communicate the intended message)... thus making them easier for an algorithm to detect and search for. That means that the replacement phrases must constantly be evolving.

prompt: How will AI change the nature of software applications and their bundling of features?
response: AI will unbundle the app. Why are apps the size they are today? Part of it is because software is expensive to write, cheap to run - high fixed cost, low marginal cost. But LLMs make software much cheaper to create. For small, non-generalized software, the cost approaches zero. When the fixed cost of writing software goes to 0, the minimum viable market for a bit of software also approaches 0. That implies that we'll see an explosion of software that is so crappy and bespoke to a particular use case that before no one would have bothered to build it. When software has a high fixed cost, the use cases have to congeal into a bundle that is big enough to have a market to make the bundle viable. But when it's zero fixed cost, those bundles no longer need to coalesce, and they also don't need to have a business model inside: the fixed cost is zero, there's nothing to pay for!

prompt: What's the difference between System 1 and System 2 thinking, and how does this relate to developing expertise?
response: System 1: cheap and fast but very specific matching. System 2: expensive and slow but general. When you have the relevant experience in your System 1 associative memory, your System 2 doesn't have to be engaged as often. Your System 2 is great at using reductionist tools; that means it's terrible at understanding complex phenomena. You have to "burn in" all of the possible situations as experiential memories, to be available for the System 1 to later draw on. If there's something unlike what you've done before, you have to engage your System 2, and it will be hard and error prone. Practice is like taking in all of the angles and remembering them so you'll be able to recognize them automatically later.

prompt: Why can't consensus drive innovation, and what does this mean for creating new standards?
response: Consensus cannot do innovation. Consensus pulls to the average. Consensus doesn't allow the isolation that speciation needs. Innovation is a defection from the average that turns out to be valuable. Therefore consensus can't create something innovative. If you want to create something innovative and novel that will ultimately be a new standard, it can't come from a pre-existing standards body. The body will take any new ideas and pull them strongly to the consensus before they have a chance to take hold in the real world. Instead, you have to create it, and then as it gains momentum and others choose to join with it and participate, it gains even more momentum. Of course, the vast majority of these experiments will fail! The standardization momentum thus accumulates *after* the original innovative act.

prompt: What is the concept of MAYA and how does it relate to innovation?
response: A useful concept: MAYA - Maximally Advanced, Yet Acceptable. There's only so far beyond what is currently acceptable that people are willing to go. The further from the status quo, the more you stick your neck out to do it. The further from the status quo, the less likely it is to work. The further from the status quo, the more costly it is. Both to execute it, but also the recovery cost if it fails, which is inherently higher. You might also have to explain your actions to everyone doing it the normal way. That can be annoying, but in some cases it can also lead to actively being punished. MAYA is the threshold that sets the bounds of the iterative adjacent possible in what kinds of ideas might work. Things that are beyond the MAYA threshold are noise; impossible to cohere.

prompt: How can complex problems be approached by product managers?
response: Often the key dynamics of a problem can be modeled as a constraint satisfaction problem. The team is only three engineers and one PM? That's a constraint. Market research has revealed that the minimum viable product must address a particular use case? That's a constraint. You have to launch something within three months? That's a constraint. In practice, a surprising number of complexities of a problem can be modeled as these concrete constraints.

prompt: What are the two additional types of constraints beyond basic concrete constraints?
response: To make constraint satisfaction more robust we need to understand two additional types of constraints: 1) concrete but initially hidden, and 2) intangible and permanently hidden. Once we do, we'll have a way to reduce complexity to the easier-to-understand complication.

prompt: What are dinosaur bone constraints and how do they work?
response: Concrete but initially hidden constraints are like dinosaur bones: hidden within the earth, and not at all obvious at the beginning. But once you figure out they're there and do the painstaking job of digging them out and dusting them off, they're concrete constraints just like the basic ones were. Once you have a good handle on that question, you've successfully dug out the dinosaur bone and the previously complex point of disagreement reduces to a straightforward constraint.

prompt: What makes laser beam constraints particularly challenging to deal with?
response: Laser beam constraints are like laser beams in a security system: impossible to see with the naked eye, and hard to remember even once you've discovered them. What's particularly difficult about laser beam constraints is that even once you've discovered them, it takes constant vigilance to avoid breaking them since it's so easy to forget about them. Even if the team has come to understand them, inevitably when a month passes without incident everyone will forget.

prompt: What happens when you violate a laser beam constraint?
response: When you violate a laser beam constraint, you'll realize something went bad pretty quickly. Perhaps a meeting that's normally productive devolves into distrustful chaos. Or a straightforward mailing list thread gets escalated and then re-escalated until hundreds of people are slinging passive aggressive jabs at one another.

prompt: What should be the focus of a company's center of excellence?
response: The core of your differentiated value--the enabler of your differential value--should be a center of excellence. Your center of excellence should be a point of pride inside and outside the company. Most of your effort should go into becoming even better at the driver of your differentiated value. For everything else, the bar to clear is not approaching perfection, but 'good enough'.

prompt: How should you approach decision-making when you don't have complete information?
response: Amazon has a famous 70% rule, that most decisions should be made with 70% of the information you wish you had. There's an asymptotic curve of usefulness of information vs cost to get it. If you're waiting to get to 90% you're waiting too long and investing too much effort. More information feels superficially like it's always a good idea, but not once you incorporate the cost of getting it, it's much less clear.

prompt: Why are games particularly effective for building expertise and knowledge?
response: Games are magic at building up knowhow. The reason games are so hyper effective is because they require the participants to be active. Instead of being able to passively watch a situation, they are called upon to make active decisions. To take actions means they have to be engaged and have a mental model of the situation. Building a robust, useful mental model is what builds knowhow. So games create knowhow as a kind of byproduct, and that byproduct can be intentionally created and harnessed in organizations by creating games.

prompt: What's the difference between rigid and adaptable systems, and when does each matter?
response: Rigid things are more efficient. But rigid things have a harder time adapting. In a changing context, adaptation is necessary to avoid death. The higher the pace layer you operate in, the more quickly the context will change, and the more important adaptation will be. Software (bits vs atoms) is at a higher pace layer than most other industries.

prompt: How should you approach building teams for ambiguous problem spaces?
response: Navigating an ambiguous problem space requires intrinsic motivation. Every fork in the road, especially when it's not clear which way to go, is an opportunity to throw up your hands and be at a standstill... or even give up. Getting someone to be intrinsically, authentically excited about a thing they weren't excited about before is extremely hard. It's easier to find people who already happen to be intrinsically motivated by that core problem (and then fan the flames) than to try to make them excited in the first place. That is, don't make people who are excited about the problem, find them.

prompt: What are the key steps to create a successful 'nerd club' or secret group for meaningful discussions?
response: Create a secret, optional group called something like "Navel Gazers." When people hear the name they should say "That sounds like a lame club for nerds." To which you respond, "Yup! …Do you want in?" This makes sure that the only people who join are intrinsically motivated to participate. In the group, set a fundamental "Yes, and" norm. It's optional and secret, so none of the discussions matter for the surrounding context. If someone says something you think is uninteresting and not worth exploring, you are free to not engage. But if you do engage, you should build on the idea. Trickle in a small number of new perspectives, continuously. Aim for perhaps 1 to 3 new perspectives added a week. This helps make sure it doesn't get stale and also the norms don't scramble with an influx of new people.

prompt: Why should you choose robustly tolerable solutions over precariously optimal ones?
response: Robustly tolerable beats precariously optimal when the downside risk is high. Robustly tolerable means a thing that is "good enough" in a diversity of realistic scenarios. It is rarely non-viable. Conditions and contexts change more often than we intuitively think they will. This means that optimized things are dangerously brittle. Efficiency is often in tension with resilience.

prompt: What is the difference between the builder mindset and the gardener mindset?
response: The builder mindset can't create more value than what it puts in. It requires a living thing adjacent to the built thing to create growth and upside. The gardener mindset is about focusing most on the living thing and seeing the built thing as secondary (e.g. a trellis). Both builders and gardeners need to rely on built things and living things. The thing that distinguishes them is: which of the two do you focus on first and foremost: the built thing or the living thing?

prompt: How can you approach innovation using lateral thinking with weathered technology?
response: One of my favorite design methodologies is lateral thinking with weathered technology. The idea is you use only technologies that have become commodity, standardized, robust, boring. Technologies you can take for granted with very little risk. Multiple providers; low cost. The innovation comes not from the components, but from how the components are duct-taped together into novel combinations. The only risk in the system comes from the duct tape for the combination, which is small and cheap. This allows you to try many different combinations quickly and with low risk. Often you can find game-changing combinations out of totally ordinary components: a kind of alchemy.

prompt: What makes taste increasingly important in a world with abundant options?
response: The more that there is a surplus of options to choose between, the more important that taste becomes. Taste is the judgment to decide between alternatives in the firehose of options. AI creates a massive firehose of content, a cacophonous background noise. In a cacophony, people will flock to the entities with the best taste. Taste is the final moat.

prompt: What is the fundamental nature of openness in ecosystems?
response: Fundamentally openness is about the existence of power differentials between entities: the ability for any one entity to exercise capricious power. The simplest real-world test is this: what is the worst case scenario if any specific entity in the ecosystem went greedy, incompetent, evil, or corrupt? How long would it take for the ecosystem to recover? In some cases, the answer is that the ecosystem couldn't recover, which is clear evidence that the ecosystem has a powerful closed portion. In other cases, the ecosystem keeps right on trucking with nary a blip — a sure sign of an open ecosystem.

prompt: How can analyzing your own position in an ecosystem be challenging?
response: We are biased to believe the best about ourselves and the goodness of our motives. But ecosystems are the sum of inter-dependent incentives, which means a proper analysis requires understanding everyone's true incentives — including your own. To do this properly, you must see yourself as others do. An easy trick to mitigate this bias is, every place that your own entity's name appears, replace it with the name of your fiercest competitor and see how you feel. That's likely similar to how others feel about you.

prompt: Why do truly open systems tend to attract more participants over time?
response: Systems that are truly open tend to attract others to join them at an ever-accelerating pace. In open systems, participants see that they don't have to fear their own success fueling their own increasing subservience to a despot. Each individual entity who can't plausibly build their own similarly-sized proprietary ecosystem to compete — the overwhelming majority of entities — is incentivized to pitch in on the open ecosystem. Investment in an open ecosystem by any one entity helps the entire ecosystem as a whole. This fact, combined with the fact that ecosystems generally get exponentially more valuable the more participants there are, means that in many cases over sufficient time scales truly open ecosystems create gravity wells, sucking more and more into them until they are nearly universal.

prompt: What are the main downsides of open systems?
response: Open systems will always be messy, chaotic, and hard to control and will never be clean, polished, or simple. In fact, the relationship between chaos and openness is fundamental: an ecosystem that gets incrementally more clean is incrementally less open. This is because an ecosystem naturally tends toward entropy over time as entities' individual incentives cause every niche of the ecosystem to be explored. Successfully influencing an open ecosystem will be like playing a four-dimensional game of chess and even the best strategies will take years to bear fruit.

prompt: Is openness inherently morally good?
response: Openness does tend to correlate with morally good outcomes. But openness is just a means to a particular end, and like any other tool, it should be judged primarily on whether it achieves good ends. Openness tends to erode the power of arbitrary power points in a system, and in many cases that leads to a better outcome for society writ large. But too much openness can erode all points of power, meaning that the entire system can become a runaway engine seeking to satisfy our basic urges with ever-increasing efficiency. As with all things, the key is finding the right balance to make sure the ecosystem is in healthy equilibrium and achieving good ends.

prompt: What are the core principles behind the Polymer toolkit?
response: The core principles are: use the platform, eliminate boilerplate, and everything is an element. Use the platform means we wanted to use the platform, but not just the existing platform, but this new platform with all these new tools. Eliminate boilerplate means you should only write the code that is unique to your app and nothing more. And everything is an element - the idea is every single thing is a module, and you use an element. You build applications out of bigger and bigger elements made of more and more elements, up to and including an actual app.

prompt: How do Web Components enable interoperability between different frameworks?
response: With Web Components, the interoperability layer is just the DOM, because an element is an element. You know how to work with it. That means that for the first time, actually, it's possible for UI elements to be interoperable. Before Web Components, you had to make everything in script and you have these conventions of what it means to be a component. Everyone's conventions have to align for them to be interoperable. With Web Components, people can align their conventions on Web Components, the standards. And then that makes them interoperable much more easily.

prompt: What is the approach with polyfills in the Polymer project?
response: The approach we're taking is building a layer of polyfills that sort of mocks out the future. A polyfill is a JavaScript library that basically mocks out a future standard so that you can use it before it actually is deployed in all modern browsers. The goal with polyfills is to have really high fidelity. So, that way, later, you don't even need it. The cool thing about polyfills is that over time, as browser vendors implement these features, the need for them goes away. And so ultimately, at some point in the future, you wouldn't even need this layer at all.

prompt: What browsers does Polymer support and why did they choose to focus on modern browsers?
response: Our compatibility chart for our polyfills includes Chrome, Firefox, IE, Safari, also their mobile variants. We call these the evergreen browsers. These are browsers that are modern, that they support many of the new standards. They also, importantly, keep their users updated by default. We're focused on modern browsers for a couple of reasons. Older browsers, you just can't polyfill some of this stuff. And really, it's time for a break. We want to move forward. And we don't want anyone who's using this stuff to be left behind, kind of like the current older browsers that don't update.

prompt: How does data binding work in Polymer elements?
response: One of the big features that we add is data binding, and this is powered by MDV. This radically reduces code complexity. MDV allows you to bind elements together and attributes together without any actual code. You can use this double mustache syntax, which is MDV, to connect properties. So you can take elements that both have a selected attribute and tie them together so when one changes, the other changes too, all without writing any JavaScript.

prompt: What are LLMs particularly good at when it comes to software development?
response: LLMs are really good at writing crappy software on demand for basically free. It's not good software often, unless you have the right structures and approaches and the right kind of scaffolding to make sure that it's doing the thing that you want it. One of their superpowers is they're really good at translation. And you can translate English to code. They do really well if you're writing it in the same kind of React style that everybody else writes it, they're really good at that because they've got tons of background information to bring into your particular situation to get really good results.

prompt: How do LLMs change the traditional cost structure of software development?
response: LLMs mess with the fundamental assumptions of software development because so much of the software industry is predicated on the idea that software is expensive to write and cheap to run. LLMs mess with both of these assumptions because code that uses LLMs to execute now has a marginal cost that is above zero, which means it is expensive in some significant way that we're not used to. And secondarily, LLMs are really good at writing crappy software on demand for basically free. These radically change how we think of where you would apply software and how you would apply it.

prompt: What is 'situated software' and how do LLMs enable it?
response: Situated software is software that is situated to a hyper-specific context. Anytime you've written a spreadsheet formula that's situated software, it's situated specifically in the context of this particular spreadsheet. Anytime that someone looks at someone else's bit of situated software, they go, 'That's a piece of crap. It barely works. It's insecure. It's ugly.' But to the person who built it, it's perfect. It does exactly and precisely what they need. And especially when LLM can help you write the situated software, you can get software that's just right. It's just the right size for you, as opposed to this one-size-fits-none software that we have today.

prompt: What are some weaknesses or limitations of LLMs in coding?
response: LLMs can't count, and it's a miracle in some ways that they work reasonably. When you have an architecture that is slightly atypical or it has some weird stylistic and calling ways it's been designed, they start getting a little bit funky there and can lose the plot. Really deeply nested JSON, they lose track of, am I in an object context or an array context? And they'll miss nest some of the things. They do a really good job of seeing patterns from common examples, but the larger sizes get increasingly less common in their training data, so they don't have as much to keep track of and they just kind of lose track of it.

prompt: Why is spending time on requirements definition so important when working with AI tools?
response: Spending time to define requirements saves extraordinary amounts of time. The requirements process is taking the serialization of the hyper object in your head and then distilling it into a more abstract and condensed version that can be efficiently communicated to somebody else. If you don't do this properly, you realize later that people didn't understand the fundamentals of the thing you're trying to do. And they went in these other weird directions and you have to tell them 'No, actually there's a whole thing. You can't do it that way. You gotta do it this way.' And they have to throw out this entire thing they just did. It's like a dynamic programming problem without memorization done in exactly the wrong order, so each calculation you're doing 20 times, 30 times or whatever.

prompt: Why do successful products tend to become more bland over time?
response: The more successful something gets, the more bland it becomes. The first step of creation only has to be viable for some set of users. But now the next step to improve it has to be viable, but also not regress anything from the previous iterations. If the next step regresses on any dimension, then some agent within the organization most responsible for that dimension will flag the issue and push back on the idea. In the limit, this push back becomes an effective veto. The person who stands to concretely lose something can make a more compelling case than the other employees who might have a diffuse, speculative benefit. This is a fundamental asymmetry.

prompt: What's the difference between consensus organizations and swarm organizations in terms of innovation?
response: An anti-consensus swarm will find novelty. A consensus swarm will become bland. An organization with a coherent identity and top-down goal will tend towards consensus. Every idea that someone might have will be checked for whether it fits with the top-down goal. Anyone in the organization who thinks a given idea will be a net negative can, in the limit, veto the entire idea. An organization that is a swarm; an emergent, bottom-up thing, will tend towards novel ideas. Members of the swarm are implicitly in competition. To compete, the members want to do the thing unlike their neighbors, to stand out and get an edge. This competition leads to differentiation, trying new things, most of which will fail, but some of which will succeed.

prompt: Why are silver bullets rare but silver swarms common?
response: Silver bullets are very rare. Silver swarms are very common. Everyone wants there to be a single, obvious, high-leverage unlock. But often the constraints are a complex web of interrelated forces; there is no single silver bullet. And wasting all of your time looking for a non-existent silver bullet will waste a ton of time. But that doesn't mean it's hopeless. Often there are silver swarms. That is, a swarm of smaller ideas or adaptations, executed by a bottom-up swarm, that collectively change the top-level characteristics. Any individual part of it looks like nothing. But the swarm overall is the secret. People can be looking right at it and not see it.

prompt: What makes communities that grow slowly more resilient than those that grow quickly?
response: Communities that grow slowly tend to be more resilient and durable. Communities that grow in a flash attract a lot of low-engagement / low-savviness members. Now your community is full of low-engagement / low-savinness users. If you ask what your users want, you will get low-quality suggestions that dumb down the product. You'll chase your worst users into the worst version of your product. If the early adopters have to go through a gauntlet, the less-engaged bounce, and only the most-engaged remain. If you ask these high-quality users what they want, they will give you interesting dimensions to develop on.

prompt: Why do people conclude that others don't care about privacy when they accept permission prompts?
response: The currently viable use cases are ones that are by construction "fine" in the current privacy paradigm. Many users simply accept whatever permission prompt they see with a shrug. But that's partially because the only permission prompts they'll ever see are prompts that someone thinks some users might reasonably decide to accept. There are implicit privacy constraints on what experiences are even conceivably viable. Because people are mostly fine with the status quo's privacy, we erroneously conclude that "people don't care about privacy." But there are use cases that are inconceivable in this privacy paradigm, impossibly creepy, unthinkable. And those are absolutely constrained by privacy.

prompt: What is the difference between allocentric and egocentric knowledge?
response: In the world of maps, "allocentric" means world-aligned, and "egocentric" means person-aligned. Before LLMs, books and media had to be aligned for a generic audience who might read them–which might not be the best format for any particular reader. That's allocentric knowledge. But now with liquid media powered by LLMs, it's possible to have the space of ideas rotate around you–what you already know, how you learn best, etc. That's egocentric knowledge.

prompt: Why might LLMs lead to an expansion of legalese rather than simplifying legal language?
response: Before, only lawyers could write and interpret legalese. That meant we landed on an equilibrium where the two sides of a contract balanced out how much legalese to use. Now LLMs can help interpret or create legalese, even for non-lawyers. The one-ply implication of this is more people can deploy and navigate legalese. But the multi-ply implication might be an expansion of legalese. The benefit doesn't go to one party; it goes to all parties. That raises the equilibrium point: a new equilibrium of misery. Now it means that anyone who doesn't use LLMs in that domain can't possibly keep up.

prompt: What is the "reverse Van Halen brown M&M" technique for testing LLMs?
response: The reverse Van Halen brown M&M for LLMs: ask it to grade the writing samples on something you know it shouldn't score highly on. For example, "is it laugh out loud funny?" If the LLM says it is, you know to look more carefully. It's an unreasonable request as a canary - if the LLM agrees to something it clearly shouldn't, then you should verify other aspects of its responses more carefully.

prompt: Why is chat not ideal as the primary interface for AI interactions?
response: Chat is too squishy to be the primary substrate. It's easy to riff with, but not great for repeatable, dependable interactions. Most things we want them to do dependably are not best expressed in chat. With chat it's very hard to emphasize some information, or show things to interact with that are easier to skim than a wall of text. A chat is just a big amorphous blob of text, hard to find a landmark. You drown in it.

prompt: How might LLMs change the relationship between humans and the speed of decision-making?
response: Humans can perceive at most about 10 bits of information per second. This is as fast as human OODA loops can go. Our whole constructed environment is designed around that 10 bits per second limit we all share. But computers have a massively faster OODA loop. Now with LLMs they can do human-style reasoning. What happens when we are no longer the fastest twitch "species"? To LLMs, humans will seem like trees - there's a mesmerizing video of an insanely fast frame rate of people on a subway platform where the people look like trees. That's how we'd look to something that has a massively faster OODA loop.

prompt: What are the three key components needed to make a system decentralizable?
response: The three components to meet the decentralizable bar are: publish immutable data, use universal identifiers, and sign everything with user-controlled keys.

prompt: How can you tell the difference between a superficially appealing idea and a truly rigorous one?
response: Most ideas get less convincing the closer you look. They start out looking snazzy or polished, but the closer you look, the more you see the gaps in reasoning, or find disconfirming evidence that undermines the simplicity of the idea. But not all things have this characteristic! Some have the opposite. They look superficially messy, random, rough and ready. But the closer you look, the more convincing they become. You see that the fundamentals are even stronger than they looked at the beginning. As you dig deeper you have more and more 'whoa!' moments, and get even more convinced by the idea. This is the mark of a truly rigorous idea.

prompt: Why should you use weaker AI models when developing prompts?
response: Use the weaker models when iterating on prompts. If you can get something to work in last-gen models (e.g. GPT 3.5 Turbo, or Claude Haiku) then it will definitely work / work better in newer models. But if you do it the reverse, you might find that it's only viable in the expensive model. The n-1 model will always be significantly cheaper than the frontier models.

prompt: What's the difference between perfectionism and nihilism, and what belief do they share?
response: Perfectionism and nihilism are thematically related. Both have a core belief of roughly 'anything that's not perfect isn't worth doing.' Nihilism adds in 'the world isn't perfect so it's hopeless'. Both are erroneous reasoning because of a smuggled infinity of perfection being achievable. Once you embrace that perfection isn't possible, you can lean into making as much good happen as you can.

prompt: How can you test whether a belief system is healthy or potentially dangerous?
response: A quick check for epistemic hygiene of a belief system: In this belief system, is doubt generative or evil? If it's considered evil, watch out, because the belief system is self-totalizing. If it's considered generative, then even if the belief system is powerful, it is likely because it can absorb and grow from disconfirming evidence.

prompt: What's the key difference between persistence and hustle?
response: Persistence is if you stick with it long enough for the non-linear benefits to kick in. Hustle is whether you keep turning the crank no matter what. But if you're turning on the crank on something that never adds up to more than the sum of its parts, then you're not getting any leverage. The question is: are the small actions cohering into something larger over time? If not, you're wasting your time on low-leverage work that has a high opportunity cost.

prompt: Why do LLMs seem to have human-like intelligence when they actually work very differently?
response: We think of it as a thing, with a complex inner world, because it can speak to us and sound human-like. This confusion leads to a category error. It feels like a human made out of machines. In reality it's more like a voice on top of a pond scum style emergent intelligence. An intelligence that is absurdly good at absorbing mind-numbingly complex patterns.

prompt: What happens when you optimize only for the individual versus only for the collective?
response: If you optimize only for the collective or only for the individual you get grotesque results at the other layer. If you optimize for only the individual, in the limit you get the super-chicken problem. Instead of building each other up and collaborating, everyone tears everyone down all the time. A hellscape of maximal competition that no value can cohere in. If you optimize for only the collective, in the limit you get a machine that churns through humans, consuming them on the path to a collective outcome.

prompt: What are the two essential skills for developing insight through self-learning?
response: The two unteachable skills for self-bootstrapping insight: 1) You see that the world is not single-dimensional and black and white, but multi-dimensional and gray. If there's something that doesn't make sense to you right now, that implies some dimension or shade of gray you can't yet sense. 2) You meet disconfirming evidence with openness and curiosity. When you see something surprising you don't lean back and say with a frown, 'oh… that's interesting…' but instead lean in closer, with excitement, and say 'oh! That's interesting!' These two skills combined allow you to be antifragile; to grow from disconfirming evidence, instead of being worn down by it.

prompt: How does the relationship between staying in one place versus moving around affect community building?
response: Stayers stay in the environment they started in. Leavers leave to find a better environment. A benefit of stayers: you build deep connections with those around you. You expect to be there indefinitely, so it makes sense to invest the time to see others around you as full-dimensional people and build trust and understanding. A benefit of leavers: you can go to your highest and best use. But if you're moving where you live every 6 months, it doesn't make sense to invest in really getting to know your neighbors. That takes time, and you'll be gone before any benefits arise out of it. People invest an amount of time and effort proportional to how long they expect to be engaged in that community.

prompt: How do web push notifications maintain security while allowing background functionality?
response: The way that it works is: you visit a site. And what we encourage developers to do is, in context, explain to users why they might want push notifications. So, say for example, you visit the Guardian. You've read multiple articles on a given topic. And they say, hey, at the end, you like this topic. Would you like to be notified when there's more articles on this topic? Well, yes. Yes, I would. You tap that on. And then, of course, Chrome verifies, hey, Guardian would love to send you notifications, just to make sure that you knew what you were doing. And at that point, if you say yes, they'll allow them to register for notifications.

prompt: What makes the web particularly appealing for beginners and tinkerers?
response: It's great for tinkerers because you don't need to get anyone's permission. You don't need to buy anything. You can just use a text editor that comes on your computer and get into it that way.

prompt: What is the key principle behind building fast web applications?
response: If you wait to the end and try to make it highly performant, it's too late. So what you really want to do is build something fast from the beginning. And as you layer in things, make sure you don't ever regress your performance.
