prompt: What were the key advantages of the web when it first grew up on desktop computers?
response: The web was really revolutionary because it helped users get access to businesses in a way that they couldn't do before with much, much, much lower friction. You didn't have to drive to the store. You didn't even have to install any software. You just opened up your browser, you clicked the link, and you were there. And that low friction is pretty key.

prompt: What are the three major pieces that were missing from mobile web experiences compared to native apps?
response: There's three major pieces. The first is connectivity. On desktop, you always had a connection. You didn't have to worry about the data dropping. But on mobile, you are constantly on the go in different conditions. The next one is push notifications. Push notifications really are just a part of the fabric of what users expect in a mobile experience. And the last one seems kind of silly, but people really don't like typing on mobile. It's just more of a pain.

prompt: How do user behavior patterns differ between desktop and mobile when it comes to purchasing decisions?
response: On desktop, it's way more goal-directed. I want to buy a couch. I'm going to go research, open up a bunch of tabs, figure out what I want to do, then maybe search for prices, and then ultimately purchase. But on mobile, it's very different. People are generally on their devices in sort of this found time. In these contexts, if you're trying to sell couches, the way to do it is not to try to get the conversion right there at that moment when someone's just waiting for their coffee. It's more about helping people be inspired by the types of things they want to buy.

prompt: What happens to user conversion rates through the typical mobile app installation funnel?
response: If you look at different stages in this funnel, you generally see that the percent that actually make it through that stage is low single digits and maybe at the highest 20%. For example, Google Plus released a case study a few years ago and showed that if users came to the site, they saw a full screen interstitial that said, hey, go get the app. And only 9% of users clicked through that. These steps compound. If you fall out of the first stage of the funnel, you don't make it to the second stage. Those low numbers actually multiply like crazy.

prompt: What results have companies seen when implementing progressive web apps?
response: When you look at how these actually impact users, across the board, in every one that we've looked at, the numbers are pretty big. We've got about 13 or 14 case studies published. And we see lots of companies are publishing their own case studies. They're just so excited by the numbers that they're seeing. And we're seeing this huge engagement, this lift in conversions, in engagement, in time spent, just capturing more of that value for users.

prompt: What's the fundamental problem with consumer businesses and their relationship to advertising?
response: Consumer businesses tend to be pulled inexorably towards engagement and ads. Larry Page and Zuckerberg originally were very against ads. Then, each found a kind of ad format that felt natural and perhaps even useful. But over time those ads fell down the gravity well of incentives into competing for users' attention. Many years ago, Google Search and Ads had a wall between them. Not any more. The wall was not bulldozed by any instantaneous force. It slowly eroded in the sandstorm of A/B experiments and tweaks.

prompt: Why is it problematic when AI model providers also control the context layer?
response: The center of gravity in this era is not the models, it's the context. There are a bunch of models of generally competitive quality and lightly differentiated abilities. The model provider should not also own the context layer. That's like the cell carrier trying to dictate you can only use their super-app on your phone.

prompt: What makes collaboration impossible when there's a huge power imbalance?
response: It's not possible to collaborate with something that's 10 times more powerful than you. The power dynamic dominates the ability to collaborate authentically.

prompt: What's the key difference between focusing on value versus values in technology?
response: We should move from value to values. Value is about what the machine thinks of you. A legible quantity that is instrumentalist and finite and fundamentally about your relation to the machine. Values are about your aspirations, your infinities, about you as an intentional human being.

prompt: What happens when people try to navigate using only maps instead of understanding the actual territory?
response: If you navigate entirely with maps and not the territory, then the creator of the map can manipulate or even harm you. They can direct you into open elevator shafts.

prompt: Why are early adopters' engagement numbers not representative of what you'll see at scale?
response: There's an inherent self-selection bias: they are by definition more engaged, more willing to try new things than the rest of the user population. This means that the beginning engagement numbers of a feature will often look better than their numbers at scale. The question for a new feature's engagement is: what portion of the engagement is due to the self-selecting effect, and what portion is structurally due to the quality of the feature?

prompt: What makes quiet threats more dangerous than noisy ones?
response: Noisy threats create an interrupt; you don't need to constantly think about them because they will spring into your attention. But dogs that don't bark you have to actively take notice of. They can sneak up on you without you noticing.

prompt: Why do cities tend to survive while companies tend to die?
response: A city is a swarm. A company is an individual. For an individual to survive, that singular, big thing must survive. For a swarm to survive, at least one small member of the swarm must survive. An individual is fragile. A swarm is antifragile. This is a massive difference.

prompt: What makes something a swarm versus an individual?
response: The difference is agency: does the entity exercise coherent agency? The amount of agency is a spectrum. Society's laws require individual humans to have agency, and holds them accountable for their decisions' repercussions. A company is also a thing that we legally allow and expect to exercise coherent agency.

prompt: How do LLMs change the programming process for formal languages?
response: Systems like code and formal DSLs have a form of built-in ground truthing for syntactical correctness: 'does this compile? Are there any errors?'. LLMs often hallucinate, but if you have a formal DSL you can automatically achieve syntactical correctness. This allows you to use LLM's indefatigability to help find things that would have been a time-consuming pain to do yourself. The important work shifts from creating syntactically valid code to verifying its semantics: less of a programmer mindset, more QA.

prompt: What is the dowsing rod metaphor for LLMs and why is it useful?
response: A dowsing rod is a fuzzy kind of imprecise 'magic' that you should hold lightly. It's also principally animated by the intuition of the operator - better intuition leads to better results. It's an object that distills and focuses that intuition into a convenient interactive package. For LLMs this "distilled intuition" works at two levels: 1) The intuition of the questioner to drive the LLM to useful insights - the most important thing to get good answers with LLMs is to ask good questions. 2) The LLM itself is the distilled intuition of all of society into a cultural technology you can talk with - the hive mind with a voice.

prompt: What's the difference between coordination and collaboration, and why does it matter?
response: Coordination and collaboration are hard for different reasons. Coordination requires ridiculous amounts of communication and paperwork. Collaboration requires hard work to create a high-trust environment for diverse perspectives to come together into something wildly better than any individual could have done themselves. Coordination is not a creative act. Collaboration is a creative act. When you create together, the creative tension helps build trust. You can kick a coordination challenge into a collaboration one by the hard work of removing the emotional, zero-sum fear - which is much easier said than done!

prompt: Why do LLMs feel like "magical duct tape" and what does this mean for finding value?
response: LLMs are magic pixie dust you can sprinkle on just about anything to make it magic. The question now is finding the richest substrate that unlocks the most value from the magic. Before, it took a lot of proprietary effort to make your thing magic. Now it requires just cheaply applying commodity magic that anyone else could apply, too. If everyone's sprinkling the same pixie dust, their magic has no differentiation. The key is finding who will use the magic pixie dust to kick off a self-catalyzing, differentiated quality loop, where the pixie dust doesn't give the magic result, but enables a self-catalyzing quality-increasing process.

prompt: What are the two unteachable skills needed for momentum in ambiguity?
response: The two unteachable skills for momentum in ambiguity are an ability to see nuance and an optimistic curiosity. An ability to see nuance means that you know the world is not single dimensional and black and white, but multidimensional and shades of gray. Optimistic curiosity is that when you find disconfirming evidence, instead of interpreting it as a threat, you see it as an opportunity, a way to gain more momentum through increased clarity. These two skills are unteachable. You either have them or you don't. If you don't have them, the only way to gain them is to be reborn with them.

prompt: What's the relationship between psychological safety and unsafe thinking?
response: True psychological safety creates the space for unsafe thinking. Unsafe thinking means looking at disconfirming evidence, actually digging into disagreements, and considering other options. Psychological safety, when done properly, can create the space for much better outcomes, because it allows rigor and ground-truthing that improves the quality of the ideas. Superficial psychological safety is about being conciliatory, never challenging anyone on their beliefs, just agreeing all of the time. This leads to brittle teams doing poor quality work. To get strong teams doing great work requires true, earned psychological safety.

prompt: How do humans typically respond to disconfirming evidence before versus after making a decision?
response: As humans we want disconfirming evidence before making a choice, but not after making it. When choosing between options we're open to disconfirming evidence, and even seek it out. But once we've decided and there's no going back and we've bound ourselves to the option we chose we become closed to disconfirming feedback. The more that it feels like there's no going back the more we don't want disconfirming evidence. This shift before and after making the choice is profound but subtle. It's easy to miss that we even change our priorities at all.

prompt: What's an effective way to triangulate and define software you want to build?
response: A great way to triangulate on software you want: compare to existing apps. When you can take known existing exemplars that are very different, you get very fast triangulation by comparing to them. For example, 'I want to make an app for cofounders to find each other.' 'OK, like tinder for cofounders' 'No, less like Tinder because the way they look doesn't matter so it shouldn't be as photos centric'. 'So more like LinkedIn for cofounders?' 'I guess, but less about strutting and resume building, more about the kinds of personality overlaps.'

prompt: What makes LLMs particularly challenging to use when you have very specific goals?
response: If you have a very specific goal with an LLM you'll have a bad time. LLMs are great when the user only knows roughly what they want. They're terrible and hard to direct when the user knows exactly what they want. They squish out unpredictably as you try to pin them down. If there's only one answer and if it doesn't get it perfectly it's hard to steer.

prompt: What level of expertise is required to effectively use LLMs?
response: LLM literacy is a thing. LLMs are not tools for lay people. They're expert tools. They're easy for anyone to pick up and quickly get surprisingly good results. But to wield them accurately and with consistently good results requires a lot of nuanced expertise. The people who can wield LLMs most effectively understand what LLMs are good at… but also understand the specific problem domain vertical they're asking the LLM about well enough to spot mistakes.

prompt: What does 'late stage' mean when describing an industry or company?
response: 'Late stage' means the finance bro vibe has infected it. You see things like: Hustle porn. 'Obviously it's all about maximizing returns in the short term. What else could possibly matter?' 'Stopping to think about implications is for losers'. The tech industry has been increasingly infected by this vibe.

prompt: What is the main tension with software created using vibe coding platforms like Lovable?
response: Software created with Lovable is often insecure. There's a tension - let's say there is a lovable, built dream journal app. You log in with your Google account, type up your dreams, mark them public or private, but halfway through, you hesitate because what if the public was the default? What if someone accidentally or intentionally coded it to leak? Suddenly, it feels less like a journal and more like shouting secrets into a dark room. It's intimate and alive software that hasn't been optimized for surveillance for scale or shareholder returns, but it reveals how little trust infrastructure we actually have.

prompt: How does Alan Watts' concept of the wisdom of insecurity relate to modern software development?
response: Watts says life is inherently uncertain and that trying to create total safety isn't just impossible, it's soul-killing. In a strange way, vibe-coded software embodies this idea. It's built by enthusiasts, full of personal expression, and fragile by default. You're not using some hardened enterprise stack, you're stepping into someone's dream world. That fragility is double-edged - on one side, it's intimate and alive; on the other, it reveals how little trust infrastructure we actually have.

prompt: What makes trust difficult in asymmetrical relationships, particularly with technology companies?
response: It's hard to have trust in asymmetrical relationships, especially when the asymmetry is invisible. The more it tilts the less you can perceive the tilt - that's the real danger, not just being outgunned but being unaware of how outgunned you are. Imagine a company that knows you better than yourself, not because it loves you, but because it's optimized you, tracked your clicks, mapped your behavior, seen you across devices, moods, years, and then imagine that same system knowing everyone else too. Suddenly it's not a relationship, it's an environment. And environments don't ask for consent.

prompt: What is the concept of hauntology and how does it apply to modern software?
response: Mark Fisher has this idea of hauntology, which refers to the idea that the present is haunted by the remnants of lost futures and the failure of the future. It suggests a sense of unease, nostalgia, and a feeling of being stuck in a perpetual present as a result of the absence of a compelling vision of the future. Hauntological software feels like it's echoing a future that never arrived - if a tool is glitchy, fragile, half usable, but emotionally resonant, it might be hauntological, especially if it mirrors early internet hope that software could be expressive, weird, personal, and not just optimized.

prompt: What is Derek Thompson's 'Anti-Social Century' and what does it reveal about technology and human connection?
response: Derek Thompson's Anti-Social Century lays out a tragedy in three acts: Act One - Bowling Alone; Act Two - Swiping Alone; Act Three - Talking To Chat Bots Instead Of People. Each decade, our tools got better while our sense of belonging got worse. Abundance without scaffolding for relationship becomes an existential famine. We have more tools than ever, but fewer real friends, more agency, less intimacy. When people call LLMs their only friend, it's not just about tech capability, it's about societal neglect. The problem isn't that chatbots are too smart, it's that our social fabric has frayed enough that this feels like the best option.

prompt: What's the difference between talking to an LLM and talking to a regular person?
response: Talking to an LLM is like talking to a choir. Or maybe a Greek chorus. Sounds like one voice but is actually a multitude singing coherently. Not a single perspective, the collective, average hive mind of humanity. Talking to everyone and no one. Haunting, larger than life.

prompt: How should we think about the role of humans when working with AI systems?
response: Without humans in the loop LLMs just produce slop. Humans are the curatorial energy that help find the greatness amid the cacophony and extract it. The right answer is not "how to have only LLMs in the loop with good output" but "how to efficiently interweave humans and LLMs in high-leverage co-creative dances."

prompt: What's the main challenge when working with LLMs?
response: The challenge with LLMs is often giving them the right context. It's not that they lack a baseline common sense, it's that they don't know anything about your particular situation if you don't tell them. Often it's hard and tiresome to tell them all the background knowledge you need them to know. LLMs are in a dark room; each conversation is a fresh start where you have to start from scratch giving it background knowledge about your particular goal and context.

prompt: Why do we end up with large, feature-heavy apps instead of smaller, simpler ones?
response: The default "size" of apps comes down to a few factors: 1) The cost to write software - the more expensive, the more features have to be bundled around the business model to make the fixed cost investment worth it. 2) The cost to distribute software - apps are high friction with big chonky installs that cost multiple dollars per install in marketing. 3) Coordination costs across apps - how hard it is for an app to coordinate with neighboring apps. The harder it is to share data, the higher the surface tension, and you'll get a smaller number of larger apps.

prompt: What's the difference between what people want and what they want to want?
response: What you want and what you want to want are often fundamentally at odds. That's how you can get trapped in a monkey trap, following your short term interest and never overriding it in favor of your long term interest. Everyone says they want disconfirming evidence, because they know that's what they're supposed to want to want. But most of the time, people don't actually want that. Capitalism can only give you what you want, not what you want to want. What you want to want is often what you need.

prompt: What's the difference between hard and soft software?
response: Hard things are efficient but brittle. They're hard to change, hard to adapt. One-size-fits-all. Software is supposed to be soft. Malleable, adaptable. Software should be like water; fluid, possible to conform to any shape. Apps are hard, like blocks. LLMs are fluid, like sand. Users shouldn't conform to software. Software should conform to users.

prompt: Why can't you add safe composability to a software system after it's built?
response: You cannot simply retcon safe, low-friction composability onto a system after the fact. The only way to have a system with safe, low-friction composition is for it to have that property from the beginning and then never lose it as you grow and extend the system. The web has maintained a security model of "clicking a link should never be directly dangerous" and "same origin isolation" since the beginning, allowing safe composition. App frameworks don't have any similar general purpose primitive.

prompt: Why do aggregators tend to centralize ecosystems even when lower layers are decentralized?
response: Even if the underlying components are decentralized, if the top layer (the way that all users access the system) is heavily centralized, then the system as a whole is effectively centralized. Aggregators aggregate the consumer experience, creating a very small number of extremely empowered linchpins in ecosystems, no matter what the lower layers do. Don't bother decentralizing the layers that don't matter to your problem.

prompt: What's the shoreline paradox and how does it relate to building formal rule systems?
response: The real world is fractally complicated. The closer you look, the more nuance you will see, another level of intricateness. Each rule costs energy to construct, test, and maintain. The value of a rule is defined by the volume enclosed. The cost is defined by the surface area. But the fractal nesting leads to many orders of magnitude more surface area the closer you look for the same volume. This is effectively the shoreline paradox. The costs scale significantly faster than the value.

prompt: What makes situated software different from traditional software?
response: With situated software, you're in a direct relationship with the software. It's not some black box built by someone else. It's software that you've evolved and grown yourself. You've been playing with it, developing your own "theory of mind" to predict what it will do, changed it to behave closer to what you want. Situated software is active software, not passive software. You're in the loop with the software.

prompt: Why are LLMs bad at handling negative instructions or removing elements from generated content?
response: LLMs don't do a good job with negative space. All of the training data has descriptions of images as they actually are. In that case, why would you describe what's not in the image? You can just describe what is in the image. But a description of an existing picture, and a description to create a picture are different. In the former, you would never use a negative word like "without". In the latter, you might, if the baseline understanding of the artist might guess that a certain detail should be included. The generative model isn't used to seeing negative/"without" words, so they're effectively background noise to it that it ignores. As you get increasingly emphatic about what to remove, it still doesn't sense the negative word, so all it sees is "wow, he seems really emphatic about this secret lair sign, I guess it should be really emphasized!"

prompt: Why do LLMs struggle with generating large, complex JSON structures despite being good at smaller ones?
response: LLMs are not doing reasoning or computation, they're doing extremely good vibes matching of things they've come across before. There's so much JSON in the training, that especially for small blobs of JSON there's tons of examples of every permutation of nesting of objects and arrays, and so its intuition / vibe matching is very resilient. But the larger the JSON blob gets and the deeper the nesting, the fewer direct examples of that exact nesting structure there is. This must be so for structural reasons, as with each step the combinatorial space grows. They aren't "counting" the parentheses, they're pattern matching based on things their attention mechanism tells them are relevant for situations like this... and those are tuned based on the stuff it's seen in training data.

prompt: What happens when you have wealth or power that others don't have?
response: Being very powerful or rich is like having an invisible mech suit. They see a problem that everyone else is unable to solve. "I would simply stomp on it and smash the problem!" And their simplistic suggestion works! As the powerful person, you're dumbfounded why everyone else makes this so overly complicated. "Why don't you simply do what I do?" They think everyone else around them isn't as smart as them. What's really happening is others aren't as strong as them, so moves that are viable for you are not viable for them, and would knock them out of the game. Strength is partially due to the amount of resources they can command.

prompt: How do bureaucratic processes differ from living, responsive systems?
response: A bureaucracy is a machine that takes alive responsibility and turns it into dead process. If there is a gap in the machine's process, a wire that is crossed or where an alert signal doesn't make it, nothing alive will run into it. A living structure and a dead one look totally similar but work for totally different reasons. The bureaucracy process morphs a living, possibly unfair process to a dead, fair one. The process is highly structured and humans are disempowered in it. No one can say "guys, the steel has rusted and no longer connects to the concrete footing, obviously shut it down."

prompt: What makes something more "alive" in terms of adaptability?
response: How alive you are is how many kinds of surprises you can successfully meet and navigate. Does your adjacent possible extend to cover that region of surprises you are likely to encounter? If a surprise shows up that is outside your adjacent possible, you have no viable responses, and you either get injured or knocked out of the game. If you've made good building blocks then you can respond to surprises and use them to tackle things you never imagined. Being composed of remixable building blocks, that are viable on their own but have combinatorial possibility, makes you more alive. Building blocks give you a combinatorial coverage in an adjacent possible.

prompt: What is the difference between focusing on immediate results versus long-term potential?
response: When we're busy, when we need results as soon as possible, we only have the space to be a builder. But when we create the space and allow ourselves to be patient, even if just for a small fraction of our day, we create the possibility for the gardener within us to blossom.

prompt: What makes acorns special compared to regular rocks or pebbles?
response: Acorns are some of the most densely packed bits of potential in the universe. They have within them the capacity to grow into a mighty, beautiful oak tree. They are seeds of greatness. A living thing has its own agency: a kind of internal magic. It is something that grows under its own power.

prompt: What do acorns need in order to grow successfully?
response: An acorn needs fertile soil, shade, and water. It needs to not be dug up by squirrels. But most importantly, acorns need space and time.

prompt: What is the best time to start investing in long-term potential?
response: The best time to plant an acorn is twenty years ago, but the second best time is now.

prompt: How can someone explain to others why they're investing time in something that seems small or unimportant?
response: When they ask you 'Why are you bothering with that pebble?' you can answer, 'Because this is an acorn,' and they can understand the magic you hold in your hand.

prompt: What's the difference between how LLMs perform on frontend code versus other types of programming?
response: One of the reasons that LLMs appear to be so resiliently good at frontend UX in modern patterns is because that code isn't challenging in a programming sense, it's a lot of boilerplate to get a mostly-the-same-kind-of-thing output. LLMs do well with contained algorithms that are commonly shared in write-ups on the internet, but the kind of random non-web-frontend glue code and architecture of a real codebase they don't do as well at. The meta insight is that LLMs' ability to write code on demand is not some smooth distribution over types of code, but spiky based on which types of code are commonly found in tutorials and open source code, as well as how boiler-plate-y the code is.

prompt: How should teams approach building prototypes when trying to find what works?
response: When you're trying to prototype to find the fun, don't overbuild. Can you duct tape something that will work for the next 3 months, and that won't set you on a path that in 3 years you'll look back and hate yourself for? If so, good enough! Low fidelity is not low quality. Fidelity is about superficial appearance. The quality is about the fundamentals. When you're searching to find something great, you want to find something that has great fundamentals but ignore the fidelity for now.

prompt: What are the key components of trust in professional relationships?
response: The amount of trust one person has in another is: Trust = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) / Self-Orientation. Credibility: Can you believe what they say? Reliability: Can you depend on them? Intimacy: Are they sensitive to you? Self-Orientation: Do they focus on themselves or the team? The higher the trust, the more you're able to navigate complex problems together.

prompt: Why might asking questions be considered aggressive in some contexts?
response: "Just asking questions" can be aggressive. Answering a question is orders of magnitude harder than asking it. Especially for complex domains, which can add a few additional orders of magnitude of difficulty. Some answers are more valuable than others. Some answers have great cost but little value. How aggressive it is is a mixture of: 1) How important is it for success for the answer to be distilled and shared? 2) How much does the questioner outrank the other person? 3) How much better at debating is the questioner?

prompt: What happens when systems don't allow for internal criticism or feedback?
response: If you can't Exit, and Voice will get you knocked out of the game, your only choice is Loyalty. A system that doesn't allow Voice as an option cannot have healthy internal self-correcting systems. It is corrupted and will only ever become more corrupt. If you're forced to do Loyalty, you might as well do it for real and get the spoils vs just pretend to go along to not get knocked out of the game.

prompt: Why is it challenging to create enhanced AI assistants with access to personal data?
response: It's extraordinarily hard, in the current security models, to make something like this (access to all of your data as input, as well as dangerous tool use) safe. I believe it requires an entirely new security model to do this well with any kind of scale.

prompt: What's the key difference between Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) and RLAIF?
response: Instead of Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) you get RLAIF. But once LLMs get enough generalized ability, they start being able to make high-quality judgment calls without a human in the loop for some tasks. If you keep it to areas the LLMs are extremely good at (e.g. domains where the LLM is so over-qualified it rarely makes a mistake) you can use RLAIF to derive a new model that is wildly better than before on that particular speciality orders of magnitude faster than if you had to have humans in the loop.

prompt: How do LLMs relate to human creativity and consensus mechanisms?
response: LLMs are inherently a kind of planetary-scale consensus mechanism. Consensus mechanisms don't produce innovation. Innovation is surprising, outside the distribution, at the edge between consensus and chaos. Consensus mechanisms can only give innovative results if there's a specific consistent bias in all of the components. An LLM is effectively a planetary scale consensus mechanism, so it's especially important to give them a specific bias in your prompt to get them to innovate.

prompt: What makes software development primarily a social rather than technical activity?
response: Software is primarily social, not technical. Like all social things, it must be grown, not built. Engineering is a social activity. Not individuals creating formally correct components separately that fit together. It's a co-creative co-ownership. Writing software together as a team is a social co-evolutionary process of generating understanding together.

prompt: What's the relationship between being busy and productivity?
response: If you want something done, give it to a busy person. When you're busy, you don't have time to say, 'just one game of candy crush first', You have to jump from one thing to the next with zero delay. It's frenetic and stressful, but you also maintain the thread of hyperfocus and activity. It's way easier to maintain that thread than to start it from scratch. More tasks can fill in the gaps and help that busy person stay busy and productive.

prompt: What's the difference between an API and a platform?
response: An API has a clear boundary; as a user you interact with it in a single, clean, self-contained interface. A platform has much less clear boundaries between it and your use case. A platform is a messy thing that can absorb the complexity of your use case and model it. A platform that is not possible to be used in messy ways won't be used widely, because real world use cases are inherently messy. The things you optimize for when building an API vs a platform are different. API: do your one task well with minimal fuss. Platform: never say no to any use case (though they aren't necessarily easy to accomplish).

prompt: Why is patience important in product development?
response: In many situations, patience is a secret weapon. The secret to Apple's deep product quality is patience. If you bake a cake for 60% of the time the box says, you won't get a cake that 60% of people want to eat, you'll get a cake that no one will want to eat! Things that grow based on intrinsic motivation are typically significantly stronger and more resilient. But you can't force a seedling to grow. The best you can do is nurture it. It can be hard to do in practice; the benefit of patience is indirect and hard to quantify while the benefit of speed is obvious.

prompt: What happens when you scale something too early?
response: At a critical threshold, laminar flow decoheres into turbulent flow. Turbulent flow is chaotic, it's many orders of magnitude less efficient than laminar flow. If you scale something too early, you cross the threshold from laminar to turbulent flow and you get chaos. Once a system is in turbulent flow, adding more energy to it just makes it more turbulent, which might even decrease the output. Adding more investment to a swirly thing will often just make it more swirly. The secret to scaling things effectively is not even a secret. It's just to be patient. Do the steps in the right order: walk, crawl, run.

prompt: How should you give effective feedback to someone?
response: Feedback is more likely to be received when it's an embrace and then a redirect. The first step is to embrace the person. Show them that you see them similar to how they see themselves, and understand and support their value and goals. Importantly, make sure to embrace them on the specific thing you'll be giving feedback on. Then suggest a new way that might help them achieve those goals more effectively. That leaves it up to them to intrinsically decide to adopt the feedback as opposed to feeling like it's forced upon them.

prompt: What is the principle of 'create more value than you capture'?
response: Create more value than you capture. This is a good rule of thumb for living in harmony with the systems and people around you. The net value you create is not just the direct outcomes, but the indirect ripple effects--the externalities. A cynical money making play that goes against this advice: profit from a given direct dynamic and externalize the downsides.

prompt: What makes certain business models more misaligned with users than others?
response: Every business model is misaligned with users in the limit. But some are more misaligned with their users. Even subscriptions see the siren's call for marginal returns of ads. An inescapable pull towards enshittification. Although perhaps the pull towards ads even with subscriptions only shows up if there's only one provider. Maybe subscription-based services that don't have any kind of content lock in could be more deeply user-aligned for longer. But having your data in a hard-to-export form is a form of lock-in.

prompt: Why is context portability important for AI systems?
response: It's critical that the model and your context are at different layers. If your context and memories are locked at one provider, you can't easily try out a new model. That means you slowly get trapped into your model's worldview. Your context must be portable, and it must not be stored at any one provider. Given how the models currently work, there's no good reason for the main UI you use–the one that stores your context/memories–to also be one of the model providers.

prompt: What is the relationship between centralization and innovation in technology systems?
response: Centralization leads to scale, and scale leads to the Coasian Floor rising. The entity with access to the data is the one who is allowed to write the code. No one but the aggregator can write the feature, and they aren't incentivized to care below a large scale. So the features aren't built, and also the broader ecosystem also can't build them. This is the tragedy of aggregation. Centralization increases the likelihood of game over of that system. It's not obvious superficially, underneath it has been hollowed out. All of its stores of adaptability, gone.

prompt: How does privacy relate to personal development and innovation?
response: Privacy is about protected spaces, a sense of self. If you don't have any private thoughts you can't escape the norms. If you can't escape the norms, innovation is impossible. The task of adolescence is to 'play yourself into being' by experimenting. Privacy isn't just about shielding people, but allowing them to unfold. On the internet kids can try out different versions of themselves. Having one context prevents experimentation and discovery.

prompt: What are the different types of efficiency that organizations need to consider?
response: A frame: adaptive efficiency. The ability to adapt and thrive, cheaply. That is, how efficiently can your system adapt? How quickly is your environment changing? The quicker it changes, the more adaptive efficiency you need. Normal efficiency is in tension with adaptability. But business people like efficiency. So reframe adaptability as a kind of efficiency to help business types understand why it's important.

prompt: What makes Claude different from other AI models in terms of personality and approach?
response: I think when you sort of play around with different models, you'll see these kinds of different strengths and weaknesses emerge. What we've heard from our users is that Claude tends to come across as friendly and warm, conversational. Claude is very apologetic. So if it won't answer a question, it'll be like, 'I'm so sorry, I can't answer your question.' Very polite. I tend to think of Claude as sort of a very like, eager junior assistant, right? It's a tool that really wants to help.

prompt: What are some of the main techniques used to make AI models safer?
response: We use something called reinforcement learning from human feedback. This is a technique we've written about. And this basically is just human labelers basically giving feedback to the models of, hey, this is a little more harmful. This is a little more helpful. But really, I think one of the kind of big breakthroughs for us over the past several months was constitutional AI. Rather than kind of scoring or giving feedback on particular responses at kind of a, you know, very high rate, you can actually instruct the model with a constitution to say things like, hey, it's not, you know, nice to, you know, be angry or mean to people. You know, you should be thoughtful about all different types of people from all around the world.

prompt: What is the fundamental trade-off when trying to make AI models both helpful and harmless?
response: There's this kind of inherent research tradeoff between helpfulness and harmlessness. You can imagine that it's actually very easy to have a perfectly harmless model. It will just not be very helpful, right? You can say, hey, can you, you know, I don't know, like write a line of code for me, or could I borrow something? And Claude will just say, I'm so sorry, I can't help you with that response. It's a perfectly harmless model. And so, really, we spent a fair amount of time kind of, especially in the late stages, you know, sort of fine-tuning between a little bit more harmful, a little bit more helpful, a little bit, a little bit less harmful, a little bit less helpful.

prompt: What are some practical use cases you've seen for AI models with very large context windows?
response: I think it's honestly often for what I would describe as kind of important, but like a little bit banal tasks. And this is sort of where this concept of a junior assistant, I think, comes in, right? If you're, you know, trying to read a lot of information, like long memos or legal briefings or many, many hours of recorded Zoom meetings or SEC filings or just earnings reports, that is a great use case that might take a human or many humans hours or days to do. And Claude can do that almost instantly. And then you can ask it information about that. What are the takeaways from the earnings reports or what were kind of the interesting things that you discovered in these articles over day X or Y?

prompt: What keeps you up at night regarding AI development and what's your optimistic vision for the future?
response: Maybe the thing I most worry about is that we have this incredible opportunity for AI to create positive change in the world. And of course, there are real risks that come along with that level of kind of transformative potential and engagement. And I think maybe, you know, the thing that keeps me up at night is just, are we as an industry, but more broadly than that, right? But if you sort of look forward another five years and imagine, well, What won't these models be able to do? prompt: Why do most bold entrepreneurial ventures fail despite having intelligent and hardworking founders?
response: If you think about it carefully you can see how those legendary heroes' accomplishments could be real and also outside the adjacent possible. Imagine a given leap beyond the adjacent possible, with a 99.99% chance of failure. One person steps up to the plate to attempt it, a perfect picture of our heroic ideal: 99th percentile grit and intelligence. But it's fundamentally a very low chance of success, so they almost certainly fail. If this happens 10,000 times, then there's a good chance that one person, by pure luck of the draw — right place, right time, on a day when all of the stars align — will succeed. That person who succeeds has effectively won the lottery. But luck only exists looking forward in time, so after they succeed we tell stories about their grit and intelligence that caused their success, ignoring the fact that everyone else who tried was also 99th percentile grit and intelligence.

prompt: What is the adjacent possible and why is it smaller than we think?
response: The adjacent possible is the set of next actions that you can take that will almost certainly work. You might say that these projects are within your grasp. But here's the bummer, the uncomfortable truth, the curse that dooms us to cycles of despair: our adjacent possibles are significantly smaller than we'd like to believe. The system of constraints operating on us at any point leave very little maneuvering room and box us in significantly. The clouds of uncertainty in the future get thicker at a compounding rate as they extend in front of us, so it's hard to see beyond our noses, and impossible to see beyond arm's reach.

prompt: How can you achieve ambitious outcomes while minimizing risk?
response: This is not a one-shot game, where you pick a single move and then put your head down and execute until you're done and see how it goes. It is an iterated game. You make your choice, you execute on it, then you make another choice. Importantly, the choices that you have after one round are different from the choices you had before. You've moved forward in the possibility space, bringing a whole new set of possibilities into your adjacent possible. You don't just make each decision randomly. Instead, you sight off of a north-star vision that you keep consistent. If you do it right, you can arc to wildly different outcomes than you thought possible when you started, perhaps even outcomes that seemed outside your cone of possibility!

prompt: What's the difference between a north star vision and a traditional business plan?
response: A north star looks superficially like a big, bold, all-in-one-chunk plan, in that it is long-term and bold. But it's unlike a plan in that it doesn't say precisely how to get there or pretend to have certainty on each step. It's fuzzy, lightly held, a navigation beacon more than a map. Your north star is typically a vision: a simple narrative that can be expressed in a page or two of succinct prose describing an outcome many years out. Everyone who reads it should come away thinking that it is at least plausible and that the outcome would be one worth celebrating if you achieved it.

prompt: Why don't more people use the iterative adjacent possible approach instead of making heroic leaps?
response: It requires embracing the inherent uncertainty. Uncertainty hurts. It's an uncomfortable feeling in the pit of our stomachs. Serious People always project certainty, so that the people who follow them don't get stuck in the abyss of nihilism and self-doubt. Projecting certainty, even if it's totally made up, is what Serious People do! By embracing the uncertainty you have to sit with an uncomfortable reality of not knowing. Other people might think you look weak and defeatist, that you aren't a Serious Person. The trick is that we don't have to have certainty in order to succeed.

prompt: What's the problem with having too short of a time horizon when making strategic decisions?

prompt: What's the difference between tailwinds and flywheels in business?

prompt: How do labels and categories limit our understanding of nuanced situations?

The bucket becomes a cage, preventing the nuance from escaping. What is real is what's in the bucket, but the bucket becomes what we coordinate on with everyone, so it goes from being a means to an end.

prompt: Why do smart leaders sometimes accidentally create echo chambers?

prompt: What is an adjacency map and how does it help teams navigate complex problem domains?

Having this shared adjacency map changes the game - now everyone can see that their use case is on the map, people can see that items on the left have fewer dependencies than those on the right, and people can also see which use cases are upstream of nearly everything.

prompt: How do meta-ecosystems differ from regular ecosystems, and what makes them powerful?

prompt: What is 'the quality that cannot be named' and how do things acquire it?

prompt: Why is the combination of illegibility and openness effective for managing downside risk when building features?

prompt: What should you do if you can't convince someone that your idea is better?

prompt: Why is diversity of approaches valuable in problem-solving?

prompt: What's the main goal of collaborative debate?
