prompt: How should you approach using LLMs in product development?
response: LLMs are kind of like having a trained circus bear in your kitchen that makes you porridge. It's a miracle that it works at all. But also, it's a wild animal that is in your kitchen and could ransack your kitchen or kill you. The porridge isn't even that good. So how do you use LLMs not as something with agency, but something that has magical duct tape? If you use LLMs as oracles, and if the oracle is wrong in a given use case, it doesn't work, it punches the user in the face, you have no recourse. If instead you use the LLMs as magical duct tape and you use it to build a kind of platform that lifts up the floor of what humans can do with their extra bit of judgment, you can have something that's much more resilient and can create value, even given that LLMs will never be perfect in every use case.

prompt: What's the difference between a builder mindset and a gardener mindset in business?
response: The builder gets immediately to work and says, 'Okay, here's some rocks I can pick up.' And here's the heaviest one I can carry and pulls it together. With sweat on his brow, he's getting stronger and more clever about how to use leverage to move rocks in place. The gardener's just sitting there, dilly-dallying and looking at stuff at his feet and looking at picking up little pebbles. The builder keeps on making more and more progress, getting stronger and more clever. But at a certain point, the gardener starts creating outcomes that start becoming large and beautiful. The secret is the gardener understands that other things can be alive, and that some of those pebbles are actually acorns. The acorn has the potential to be alive and to be this big, massive thing that grows on its own. It takes a little bit of tending and care and finding the acorns and watering them and putting them in good soil. But that looks very unlike building that entire tree yourself.

prompt: What is systems thinking and why do people find it challenging to apply?
response: Systems thinking is almost defined by its multi-ply thinking, which allows you to call non-systems thinking one-ply, which brings to mind rest stop toilet paper. The problem with multi-ply thinking is it's extremely hard to coordinate multiple individuals, especially in a cacophonous background where everyone's running around doing stuff. It takes time to think through the implications - they're not obvious. And if you get any of the earlier plays wrong, your entire conclusion is incorrect. People who understand systems theory too early in their career find it's really a curse because you'll see extra steps ahead of what your boss sees. The number one rule of all organizations is pretend your boss is right because your boss can fire you. If you understand something they don't understand and you're doing something to avoid a problem they don't see, it can be a real problem because they'll say no, simply do X, but X will cause the opposite of the thing that we want to happen.

prompt: How do you optimize for serendipity in organizations?
response: The general technique is what we call optimizing for serendipity, farming for miracles. What you do is you plant a small number of diverse set of acorns. Acorns are small little ideas that are viable, that might turn into grow into something. And then you just invest energy in ones that tend to happen to grow. So the ones that don't grow, you don't fret about, you don't try to force them to work. You just plant a whole bunch. And then respond to the ones that are going in the direction you want. And don't hold on too tightly to any particular plans. You're creating the wave that you were surfing to some degree. If you do this properly and you plant a diversity of seeds, and they're all cheap enough to have very little downside - the only downside is opportunity costs - you can get pretty good coverage of a number of interesting frames.

prompt: How should you think about using LLM coding assistants most effectively?
response: LLM coding assistants are best thought of as interns. You'd never have the intern design the architecture and API of a project. But you would have them do the implementation. That's the best way to use LLMs for code, too.

prompt: What happens to systems that become over-optimized?
response: Systems that are over-optimized get hollowed out. Superficially they are thriving, doing even better than ever before. But inside they are hollow; zombies shuffling forward. Power is centralized, everything is overly efficient, adaptability is lost. A system that is alive can adapt, plan multiple steps ahead. A system that is a zombie can only shuffle forward and plan a single step ahead.

prompt: Why do systems need noise to adapt effectively?
response: Systems need noise to be able to adapt. Imagine a bullseye that lots of archers are trying to hit. It's natural for a team to try to optimize the accuracy of their archers. But now imagine the lights turn out, everything is totally dark. If the target moves, you have to probe in the dark, sending arrows randomly to try to find a hit. If you would have had some noise in the arrows, some spread around the bullseye, the chance is that one of the arrows would have kept hitting. That would have shone the way for the other archers to update their aim. This noise fundamentally allows sensing in the dark.

prompt: What's the key difference between demoing a tool and actually using it?
response: Demoing a tool and using it are fundamentally different stances. Demoing is about meeting the tool where it is - using the tool as an end. If the demo works it's upside. But real use is about using it as a tool to accomplish things you care about. The tool is entirely a means. If the tool doesn't do what you want it's a bad means. You discard means that don't work. A threshold is cleared when a team building a tool starts using it for real use, not just to demo it for themselves.

prompt: How can you balance automation with human control to prevent runaway systems?
response: The compounding domino model of human interaction follows a principle: every action that could have irreversible side effects outside the system must be initiated by a human. This prevents automation run amok. You can add layers of leverage that then actuate the layers below. As the user gets a better handle on the quality and correctness of a given interface, they can decide to add a layer that actuates it, too. The key thing is 1) the human always kicks off the chain reaction, and 2) each new layer is one the human chose to put in place, with calibrated confidence in the interactions of the layer below.

prompt: What is the fundamental problem when building products or services with network effects?
response: If you have an ecosystem underlying your product or service, getting that self-sustaining flame is likely your fundamental problem. Without that thriving ecosystem, your product is probably worthless.

prompt: What's the main issue with using a flamethrower approach to launch an ecosystem?
response: Flamethrowers have one big problem. While you're using them, it's tempting to see all of that flame and think, 'Look at all of that fire! My ecosystem is thriving!' But at some point you will run out of fuel and have to turn off the flamethrower. If none of the logs caught on fire — if you haven't created the self-sustaining flame — then you've failed. And it's basically impossible to tell if the wood caught without turning off the flamethrower.

prompt: Why is it difficult to predict whether an ecosystem will succeed?
response: It's impossible to say, because every bonfire is different. In some cases the wind might be the most important factor. In other cases the wood might be too damp. Perhaps you needed more fuel than you thought. In general it is impossible to predict a priori whether or not your bonfire will catch; the forces, although sometimes obvious in retrospect, are too subtle and interrelated to untangle ahead of time.

prompt: What's the recommended approach for building a developer library ecosystem?
response: Develop the whole thing in the open, from the very first commit. Every meaningful discussion, every difficult decision, every embarrassing setback, should be played out in public. Start out fully obscure: no logos, no brand names, no fanfare. Over time, informally share the link with folks you think might be interested. When others start contributing — on the mailing list, in issue discussions, in pull requests — make them feel welcome and incorporate their input.

prompt: What makes a healthy ecosystem valuable?
response: A healthy ecosystem is a roaring bonfire. Once it gets going, it keeps growing on its own with self-sustaining momentum. That's the wondrous property of a healthy ecosystem: it can produce benefits way greater than the investment put in by its creator.

prompt: What's the best approach for choosing which projects to invest in when you have many possibilities?
response: One approach is to first sit with the problem, almost communing with it. Taking the time for the cloud of uncertainty to lift, just a bit. You should spend at least some time looking for novel, disconfirming evidence. You might find a great pathway you hadn't considered. Then, you look for cheap projects that have the strongest activation gradient (marginal value / marginal cost) and also bring you at least incrementally more in the direction of the north star. This research is broader, more exploratory, more based on intuition and sensing. You might call this approach 'novelty search with vibes'. This approach goes slower in the short-term, but is more likely to discover long-term viable/fast paths.

prompt: How can you earn loyalty from a counterparty in business relationships?
response: Multiple times, proactively give them something you know they will value before they even ask for it. This demonstrates to them that you are proactively looking out for them and not treating them transactionally. The signal is even stronger if the thing you give them has some direct cost to you.

prompt: What's a major benefit of open source development compared to traditional product marketing?
response: You don't have to assert that it's good enough; users can decide that for themselves. When you market a product, you're essentially asserting 'I believe this tool is at least good enough for your use'. Sometimes that's hard to distinguish a priori, and if you get it wrong you can significantly erode trust. But often your product is 'good enough' for some motivated subset of your users well before you realize it. Open source development allows a thing to not be marketed, and yet still have the possibility of self-selecting users using it anyway.

prompt: Why is it important for leaders to acknowledge fundamental tensions in projects?
response: If as a leader you don't acknowledge a fundamental tension, then you've set up your subordinates for failure. The task is secretly impossible and yet your subordinates are responsible for delivering it. If you're distracted or busy, when the project fails it's easy to erroneously conclude not 'the idea is wrong' but 'the execution was wrong'. The people best situated to understand disconfirming evidence (e.g. how hard a given task actually is) are those executing on it, developing knowhow. That's why seeking disconfirming evidence from subordinates before committing to a plan is the best way to make sure you understand the forces in tension.

prompt: How should you think about past decisions that seem wrong in hindsight?
response: Be compassionate to your past self for decisions you made. You did it in an environment that was way more uncertain than today! It's way way easier to make decisions after the fact, because now you know how some of the wave functions will collapse. The view from the future looking back is on a relative basis practically omniscient. It's common to have been right when you made the decision (based on your bets in uncertainty) and wrong now (based on how the world has evolved, and what things have now become more clear). The correctness of your decision is not static; it changes as the conditions change.

prompt: Why are LLMs inherently bland and how can you make them more interesting?
response: LLMs are inherently bland because they only know how to do the average, safe thing, the most regression-to-the-mean thing within the frame you give it. However you can get LLMs to say interesting or piquant things. To do so, you have to give them an interesting frame. They'll still be bland within that frame, but the result is still interesting if the frame is interesting. The frame comes from outside the LLM - you use an external force to push it off its balance and into a new catchment area.

prompt: What's the difference between how LLMs and humans process information?
response: LLMs don't reason, they intuit. With enough scale, this can do an extremely convincing facsimile of reasoning. LLMs appear to be possibly incapable of original reasoning, but so good at hyper-powered fuzzy intuition at scale that they can do a shockingly good facsimile. Humans are capable of reason, but the vast, vast majority of the time we do what LLMs do - we use a cached good-enough reasoning answer via hyper-powered fuzzy intuition. Our reasoning center is extraordinarily expensive, and we'd rather not use it very often.

prompt: What's the difference between primary and secondary use cases for products?
response: A primary use case is one whose expected value for a user exceeds their expected cost. If for a user the expected value is greater than the expected cost, then there is some activation gradient; the tool is viable and over time will diffuse along that gradient and become adopted. A secondary use case is one that is not strong enough to be a primary use case, but is a nice bonus. If a user is already using the tool for a primary use case, and they see out of the corner of their eye a secondary use case that is relevant, they might use it. Some secondary use cases have such a strong network effect that over time they grow to eclipse the primary use case and become their own primary.

prompt: What is the tyranny of the marginal user?
response: The tyranny of the marginal user is about regression to the mean. The marginal user represents the gradient towards the most bland thing. If you chase that gradient, you will speed along the gradient towards the most bland, mundane, same-y thing - the maximal regression towards the mean. You arrive at the local minima of the basin of the given domain. A gravity well for all use cases that start anywhere in that basin.

prompt: How can LLMs be used as a foundation for building systems?
response: The power of the LLM sets not a ceiling on what is possible, but a floor. A human could have done all of the work, but that likely would be below the cost/value curve and not viable. The LLM's starter answer provides a floor that lifts significantly more use cases above that viability threshold. Design a system with humans in the loop - the whole system should be possible for humans to do, even if it's a lot of work. LLMs then provide some good-enough starter ability. The LLMs are the floor and the height of that floor is how good the LLMs are. The higher the floor, the less humans have to do, but humans can always do as much as they want, and reach for the sky.

prompt: What's the most important characteristic of AI agents?
response: The most important characteristic is that the AI can loop on tool use between synchronization points with the user (i.e. between conversation turns).

prompt: How should you use LLMs for writing tasks?
response: Don't use an LLM to write for you, use it as a thinking partner. LLMs' ideas are never good - they're always mush, just frog DNA. But LLMs do give you a fun, well-read conversation partner to bounce your ideas off of which helps make your ideas better. LLMs have good bounce; they're willing to entertain whatever line of inquiry you want to engage in. The bouncing back and forth of ideas is how they become better. The discussion helps make your writing output better, even if no specific line was written by the LLM.

prompt: What's the difference between automating suggestions versus automating behaviors?
response: It's easier to synthesize suggestions than to automate behaviors for users. A lot of companies are focusing on automating annoying tasks like booking a flight. But those have significant downsides if you get them wrong! If the system books the wrong non-refundable $1000 flight for you, that's a huge deal. But what's more useful is gathering a lot of data for the user in one place and sifting through it to help present the user with high-quality options that they can then execute themselves with a few clicks if they like them. That way you get the benefit of the patience of the AI system sifting through details, but still rely on the intention and execution of the human.

prompt: Why does the vertical SaaS business model work so well?
response: The vertical SaaS playbook is unreasonably effective. It works because having good-enough components pre-wired together well for a niche is better than having great components that customers have to duct tape together and configure themselves. It turns out there's basically no way to do arbitrary integration cheaply or scalably. Vertical SaaS gives a super-linear return because it gives a one-size-fits-all solution to a given niche. All of the businesses in a given vertical are more or less the same. Then the hyper-stickiness of being the system of record or 'operating system' for the customer gives durable margin.

prompt: What's the key insight about comparing options at different levels of development?
response: When considering options, don't compare options of differing levels of fidelity. Each order of magnitude of fidelity requires an order of magnitude of work to accomplish. Ideas only get less pure as you develop them to higher fidelity. If you compare one option that is at high-level sketch, it will look pure, clean, and promising, especially compared to a messy, bug-ridden prototype. But the work to create the extra fidelity in the former will almost certainly make it messy, too. Pick the pre-existing thing that is the highest level of fidelity that is a good enough match to your specific problem domain.

prompt: What makes a system hackable?
response: The definition of hackable system: an enthusiast can crack it open, make an exploratory change, and get something that works again.

prompt: How do LLMs change programming accessibility?
response: LLMs unlock another few orders of magnitude of programmers. Not everyone will be a programmer, but many more will be willing to learn. Deciding to try it is an expected cost vs expected value calculation. What they see other people doing changes that calculation of expectations.

prompt: What is the key use case of LLMs?
response: "Have conversations with large amounts of data" is the key use case of LLMs. Computers are patient, but not subtle. Now LLMs learned how to be subtle and savvy, and they're still patient! Data doesn't know how to synthesize itself to be something humans can interact with directly. But now LLMs can!

prompt: What's the difference between automation and augmentation?
response: Automation is about handing over agency to the machine. Augmentation is about getting more and more leverage for your actions as a human. An exoskeleton vs a butler.

prompt: Why do platforms sometimes destroy what made them successful?
response: People sometimes kill the goose that lays the golden eggs because right at the moment you get a very nice goose dinner! If the golden eggs are hard to measure (illegible) then it's easy to get in this situation. Often the connection between the goose and the golden eggs is way, way less obvious and more indirect. In the flurry of activity and chaos, it's easy to see the value of the goose dinner, harder to see the abstract and indirect value of the possibly golden eggs in the future.

prompt: What's the difference between a builder mindset and a gardener mindset when approaching problems?
response: So much of the way that we tackle problems and build products is this builder mindset. It's like, I have a plan, I then manipulate things to match my plan and make it happen, and this is a way you can create tons of value. Part of the problem though is it can't possibly create more value than the effort that you put into it. What I look for instead are things that can be gardened, things that can grow on their own and that you can sort of direct or maybe give a little bit of extra energy to or curate over, and it's a totally different mindset for it. If you do this properly, it looks like magic.

prompt: How should product managers think about using LLMs and generative AI in their work?
response: I think they're truly a disruptive technology. To me, LLMs are magical duct tape. They are formed principally by the distilled intuition of all of society into a thing that operates in a cost structure between a human and plain old computing. So much of how the industry is built presupposes the idea that software is expensive to write and cheap to run. And LLMs undermine both of these. So it makes it LLMs allow writing shitty software to be significantly cheaper. That's really good software, but like, you know, good enough in certain contexts. And also, it means that there's certain software now that isn't plain old computing that can be run cheaply.

prompt: What is organizational kayfabe and why does it emerge in companies?
response: Kayfabe is a word that comes, I believe, from a carny word that is used and applied to professional wrestling. And it means a thing that everybody knows is fake, and yet everybody acts like it's real. And I think it's one of the defining forces within an organization, any organization. Kayfabe in the small is optimism, enthusiasm. The problem that you get is as organizations get larger, imagine you are five levels down or something and you have this project you're working on, and you're trying to give a status update to be rolled up to leadership. This happens up multiple layers and it compounds. So then your layer above you does the same and the same and the same and levels up. You can be many orders of magnitude off of the ground truth.

prompt: What advice do you have for building productive communities or discussion groups?
response: Communities are all about momentum. You want to have a space too small, a time too short. What you're doing is you want the smallest seed of people that you know are going to be actively engaged. So maybe there's four of you that already talk over lunch. You talk about whatever topic. It's always really interesting and generative. Cool. Get that group together and do a thing. And then incrementally add people who you think are going to like that already as it currently exists. And then you need to feed it. So you want to make sure that it never dies. The community that has only one person talking is a community with no people talking is definitely dead. A community with one person talking is already dead.

prompt: How can someone balance having ambitious goals while managing expectations?

I'm saying, Hold those expectations lightly, allow them to change, be willing to be convinced by different things and seek something great.

prompt: What is the key mindset shift needed for platform development?
response: I think the simplest way in the core of it is the gardening mindset. As opposed to the builder mindset. The builder mindset is highly engineered, highly focused on control. prompt: What are the fundamental security problems with using LLMs to make security decisions?

If you say "use an LLM to make a security decision" you're already hosed... no matter how many layers you nest it. It's turtles all the way down! Having an LLM make security decisions is like allowing your grandpa to give out your home address to every spam caller 'so they know where to send the prizes.'

prompt: Why don't we see many code injection attacks today, and how does this relate to prompt injection?

Prompt injection cannot be solved by mechanistic approaches like vanilla code injection can. Don't confuse the lack of prompt injection attacks with a lack of demand. prompt: What makes LLMs fundamentally different from truly creative systems?

prompt: What is the core problem with combining foundation models and user context under one entity?

prompt: How has the traditional business model of the open web been fundamentally broken?

prompt: What's the key difference between traditional software engineering and building applications with LLMs?

prompt: How do LLMs change who can create software?

prompt: What happens when the cost of creating software becomes extremely low?

prompt: What is the difference between the universe of imaginable software and viable software today?

prompt: What is the concept of 'situated software' and why is it important?

prompt: What is RAIL in web performance and what do the letters stand for?
response: RAIL is basically a conceptual framework for knowing when you have good enough performance. The key insight is, when is performance good enough? When the user can't perceive it, that's when it's good enough. The first one is R for reaction time - you have 100 milliseconds to do something, to show you got it when the user puts their finger down on the glass and starts interacting. Next one is animation time - you've got 60 frames per second as the gold standard, which means 16.67 milliseconds. Then there's idle - you want to make sure you chunk up your work that you do even in idle so if at any point the user starts interacting with you, you're good to go and can hit that 100 milliseconds. And the last one is load time - the gold standard here to keep the train of thought solid is one second.

prompt: What is a Service Worker and how does it work?
response: Service Worker is basically a client-side proxy written in JavaScript. The basic idea is when you visit a site, you can install a Service Worker. And that Service Worker is basically a script off to the side. And now, when the browser is going to fetch a resource from your domain, it first, instead of going to the network, it asks the service worker, hey, what do you want to do? And the service worker can say, just do what you would have done. Go to the network, please. Or it can say, please hand back this thing from the cache. Or it can even say, create a resource on the fly and pass that back. This allows you to build arbitrarily complex offline experiences.

prompt: What's the best way to think about using LLMs in your work?
response: LLMs are best used as a thought partner, not as an oracle. They will often be wrong; you should assume anything they tell you is likely to be approximately right, but perhaps wrong in the details. But if you use them as a generative thought partner, they can be very generative!

prompt: How should you approach conversations when trying to share new ideas with others?
response: Your conversational partner is not a podcast. Be willing for the conversation to move a notch slower than the fastest you could comprehend, if it means the other person stays along for the ride. The cadence of a conversation needs to be set by the carrying capacity of the slowest partner. You will be able to understand your own ideas an order of magnitude faster than others, because you already understand them!

prompt: What's the relationship between agency and making a positive impact?
response: Agency is making the world different than it would have been without you. Notably agency and goodness are not necessarily aligned. Agency is recognizing you can make a difference. You can't change the hand you were dealt but you can change how you play it.

prompt: How do you know if an idea has potential to be successful?
response: A general rule of thumb for compelling ideas: a number of different people who look at it go, 'that right there could be great.' The more people who have that reaction to start, and the more diverse their backgrounds, the more likely it is to be great.

prompt: What's the key difference between hobbyist engineering and production engineering?
response: You can be a great hobbyist engineer and a terrible production engineer. A hobbyist engineer just needs to find something scrappy that works - it doesn't matter how it works, just that it works. But a production engineer needs to find something that is maintainable, that multiple people can collaborate on and fix bugs in, even when things are on fire, that can scale and be explainable. The things that make production engineering hard are of a totally different character from the things that make hobbyist engineering hard in the first place.

prompt: What's the difference between how we should think about LLMs versus traditional oracles?
response: Remember: treat LLMs less like oracles and more like muses. Insights that "sound good" are what LLMs give. Which means you have to be careful, because it's giving you a consensus answer, which might be wrong.

prompt: How do social sifting processes create quality outcomes?

When there's a consistent bias to the collection actions so the average of the noisy input leads to the bias popping out sharply. The bias can be things like "many people seeing this would find it useful."

prompt: What's a key principle for automating tasks with AI to maximize success?
response: Don't automate important tasks users do today; automate things they don't bother with today. If you automate important tasks they do themselves today, anything less than perfect could be below the bar of "good enough." But there are likely some use cases that the user would get some value out of but it's not worth the effort for them today. If you can automate those, then you create new value that wasn't possible before. If you fail, the user is no worse off than they are today. All upside, no downside.

prompt: Why do software pipelines become fragile as they get more complex?
response: Pipelines are very sensitive to the weakness of their components. The chance the entire pipeline works requires multiplying the likelihood of any of the subcomponents failing. All it takes is one component in a pipeline not working to invalidate the whole pipeline. With a high failure rate of components or a long pipeline, that error rate of the pipeline goes up very quickly.

prompt: What determines whether users will evangelize a product to others?
response: The willingness of a user to evangelize is tied to wow moments more than the total value. It's possible for a tool to deliver lots of value but with lots of small savings constantly. That's a product that a user might love and be unwilling to give up, but probably wouldn't actively evangelize to their friends. Contrast that with a service that has the same amount of value, but more spiky. Rare spikes of standout "wow" moments. Those wow moments are prominent, easy to describe to others, and hard to ignore. That makes them more obvious to evangelize to others.

prompt: What's the difference between Google's 20% time and your concept of 120% time?
